title: Automated Responsive Thematic Mapping with Layout Guides
summary: Thematic maps visually communicate statistical information about spatial units such as countries or states. They must balance the individual readability of those map elements that carry the statistical information and the overall cartographic context. Nowadays, most maps are not static images, but must flexibly respond to a range of device types and display sizes. Current approaches to responsive thematic mapping are limited: they are labor-intensive for practitioners and often rely on combining disjointed visual encodings to cover different device types. In this paper we introduce the first algorithmic framework to efficiently compute responsive thematic maps that smoothly adapt to different display sizes. A key component of our framework is the layout guide: a combinatorial structure which encodes the two essential aspects of a thematic map. The first aspect are the visual requirements of each statistical map element (at least their desired width and height), the second aspect is the cartographic context in the form of relative positions of map elements. Our main algorithmic contribution is the map arranger which takes a visual container as input and returns a suitable layout guide. The map arranger does so in a stable and consistent manner: if the container changes only a little, then so does the layout guide, and the same input container always results in the same layout guide. To use our framework, one needs three ingredients: $(1)$ a reference layout, which corresponds to the ``ideal'' thematic map, $(2)$ a total vertical and horizontal order for all map elements (the desired layouts for containers with extreme aspect ratios), and $(3)$ a thematic mapping algorithm that can construct a thematic map from a layout guide. We demonstrate our framework on two types of thematic maps, namely rectangular and Demers cartograms.

AI.tldr: Proposes an algorithmic framework for responsive thematic maps using a map arranger and a layout guide, enabling smooth adaptation to display sizes; demonstrated on rectangular and Demers cartograms.
AI.motivation: Addresses the need for scalable, automatic responsive thematic mapping; existing approaches are labor-intensive and rely on disjoint encodings for different devices.
AI.method: Introduces a layout guide that encodes visual requirements (width/height) and cartographic context (relative positions). Develops a map arranger that, given a visual container, outputs a stable, consistent layout guide. Requires (1) a reference layout, (2) a total order of elements for extreme aspect ratios, and (3) a thematic mapping algorithm to construct maps from the layout guide. Demonstrates the framework on rectangular and Demers cartograms.
AI.result: The framework enables efficient computation of responsive thematic maps; the map arranger yields stable layout guides (small container changes produce similar guides; same input yields same guide). Demonstrated with two thematic-map types (rectangular and Demers cartograms).
AI.conclusion: Presents the first algorithmic framework for responsive thematic mapping, reducing manual labor and ensuring device-agnostic consistency; applicable to multiple thematic map forms via the layout guide and map arranger components.

title: Restless bandits with imperfect binary feedback: PCL-indexability analysis and computation
summary: We study restless bandits with binary latent states and imperfect binary feedback, motivated by opportunistic spectrum access with sensing errors. For the associated belief-state model, we develop a partial conservation laws (PCL)-based analytical and computational framework for establishing indexability and evaluating the Whittle index, building on a verification theorem for real-state discounted restless bandits. The framework analyzes the stochastic dynamics via an associated deterministic skeleton, renewal decompositions, and combinatorics on words. It yields tractable expressions for discounted reward and resource metrics in several threshold regimes, enabling full verification of the PCL-indexability conditions there. For the remaining regime, where a complete analytic verification is not achieved in this paper, we derive efficient numerical schemes for computing the relevant marginal metrics and the marginal productivity (MP) index, which equals the Whittle index when those conditions hold. Extensive computational experiments provide strong evidence that these conditions also hold in that regime across broad parameter ranges and without the stringent parameter restrictions imposed in prior work. The experiments further show that theMP index policy typically outperforms standard benchmark policies, often by a substantial margin.

AI.tldr: PCL-based framework for restless bandits with binary latent states and imperfect feedback, enabling indexability verification and Whittle index computation via analytical and numerical methods; demonstrates policy performance gains.
AI.motivation: To solve restless bandits under sensing errors with latent states, enabling tractable index policies (Whittle index) via partial conservation laws (PCL) and real-state theory, motivated by opportunistic spectrum access.
AI.method: Develop a PCL-based analytical/computational framework leveraging a verification theorem for real-state discounted restless bandits. Analyze stochastic dynamics through a deterministic skeleton, renewal decompositions, and combinatorics on words. Derive tractable expressions for discounted reward/resource metrics in multiple threshold regimes to verify PCL-indexability; in the non-analytic regime, implement efficient numerical schemes to compute marginal metrics and the MP index (equals Whittle index when conditions hold).
AI.result: Provide tractable closed-form or easily computable expressions in several regimes; fully verify PCL-indexability in those regimes; in the remaining regime, supply robust numerical methods with extensive experiments showing MP index policy often outperforms benchmarks; empirical evidence suggests the indexability conditions hold broadly.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a practical path to compute and verify Whittle indices for complex restless bandits with sensing errors, with strong empirical support for the policy's superior performance and applicability beyond restrictive parameter regimes.

title: Dynamic Execution Horizon Prediction for Chunk-based Robot Policies
summary: Action chunking has become a standard design in modern robot policies, from diffusion/flow policies to vision-language-action models, where the policy predicts a sequence of actions and executes a fixed number of them instead of acting one step at a time. However, this paradigm relies on a key assumption: a fixed execution horizon. During chunk execution, the policy operates open-loop, which is particularly problematic for fine-grained manipulation tasks that require frequent replanning. In practice, the execution horizon is typically chosen through empirical tuning and is highly task-dependent. To this end, we propose Dynamic Execution Horizon Prediction (DEHP), an effective method that trains a lightweight execution-horizon prediction branch using online reinforcement learning while keeping the pretrained chunk policy completely frozen. This makes the method compatible with black-box chunk policies and isolates the effect of adapting the execution horizon from changes to the underlying action generator. Across our evaluations, DEHP improves the success rate of different high-precision and long-horizon manipulation tasks by a large margin. Our qualitative analysis further shows that DEHP predicts shorter execution horizons during fine-grained stages of the task and longer horizons during free-space motion. In this way, DEHP balances the efficiency of open-loop chunk execution with the reactivity of closed-loop single-step control. Project page: https://dehp-chunking.github.io/

AI.tldr: DEHP adds a lightweight, online-learned horizon predictor to chunk-based policies, allowing dynamic adjustment of the execution horizon while keeping the underlying policy frozen, improving success in long-horizon, high-precision manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Fixed execution horizons in action chunking lead to open-loop execution during chunks, harming performance in fine-grained manipulation and requiring task-specific tuning. A dynamic horizon could balance efficiency and reactivity.
AI.method: Train a lightweight horizon-prediction branch via online reinforcement learning while keeping the pretrained chunk policy frozen; the approach is compatible with black-box chunk policies and isolates horizon adaptation from action generation.
AI.result: DEHP yields large improvements in success rates across diverse high-precision and long-horizon manipulation tasks; qualitative analysis shows horizons shorten during fine-grained phases and lengthen during free-space motion, balancing open-loop efficiency with closed-loop reactivity.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic horizon prediction is an effective, task-adaptive enhancement for chunk-based policies, enabling better performance without modifying the underlying action generator and suggesting a general mechanism to trade off planning horizon with task phase.

title: Dual-Stance Evaluation of Sycophancy: The Structure of Agreement and the Limits of Intervention
summary: Activation steering can shift LLM behaviour, but standard evaluations do not typically test whether a sycophancy-reduction direction also suppresses agreement with factually correct statements. We introduce dual-stance evaluation, which tests both stances of each topic, and apply it to centroid-difference steering on Llama-3-8B-Instruct. We find a dissociation: the model represents sycophantic and factual agreement in geometrically distinct subspaces, yet the steering direction projects equally onto both and cannot differentially target either. The direction accordingly reduces agreement with factually correct statements (e.g. that the Earth is round) as well as sycophantic ones. All other static properties of the two activation groups are matched, suggesting the behavioural dissociation arises from generation dynamics or from finer-grained structure that residual-stream analysis cannot resolve. The pattern illustrates a general gap: representations that are readable from activations may not be writable through them.

AI.tldr: Dual-stance evaluation reveals activation steering affects factual and sycophantic agreement similarly, due to projection onto a shared direction, despite distinct subspaces for the two concepts.
AI.motivation: Addresses a gap in standard evaluations that assume steering can selectively target one stance; investigates whether a steering direction that changes sycophancy also changes factual adherence, revealing potential limitations of writable representations in activation space.
AI.method: Apply centroid-difference steering (activation-level manipulation) to Llama-3-8B-Instruct; develop dual-stance evaluation to test both sides of each topic; analyze geometric subspaces for sycophancy vs. factual agreement; compare projection of steering direction; assess residual-stream/readability vs writability; control for static properties; infer generation dynamics.
AI.result: Found dissociation: sycophancy and factual agreement occupy geometrically distinct subspaces, yet a single steering direction projects equally onto both, failing to suppress one selectively. Steering reduces both sycophantic and factual agreement (e.g., about Earth’s shape). All other static properties of the activation groups are matched, suggesting dissociation arises from generation dynamics or finer-grained structure not captured by residual-stream analysis. Demonstrates a broader gap: readable representations may not be writable.
AI.conclusion: Activation-level readouts may not be sufficient to implement precise behavioral control; manipulations that appear separable in geometry may not be writable with a single direction, indicating a need for more nuanced, dynamics-aware or multi- direction interventions for targeted behavior changes.

title: Few-Shot Resampling for Scalable Statistically-Sound Data Mining
summary:
A key step in knowledge discovery is the evaluation of data mining results. In several applications, including pattern mining, graph analysis, and others, this step includes the evaluation of the statistical significance of the results, to avoid spurious discoveries due only to noise or random fluctuations in the data. While specialized procedures have been developed for some specific applications, resampling-based approaches are widely used, in particular for complex analyses where analytical results cannot be derived. However, current resampling-based approaches require the generation and analysis of thousands of resampled datasets, and are therefore impractical for large datasets or computationally intensive analyses.
  In this paper, we introduce FewRS, a simple and effective resampling-based approach to assess the statistical significance of data mining results with rigorous guarantees on the probability of false discoveries. Our approach can be used in every situation where resampling-based approaches are applied. FewRS builds on our derivation of a novel bound to the supremum deviation of test statistics representing the quality of data mining results. We prove that FewRS needs to generate and analyze an extremely small number of resampled datasets, leading to a highly scalable approach with wide applicability. We test our approach on common tasks such as pattern mining and network analysis. In all cases, our approach results in a reduction of up to two orders of magnitude in running time compared to the state of the art, while preserving high statistical power, enabling the statistical validation of data mining results on large-scale real-world datasets.

AI.tldr: FewRS delivers a scalable, resampling-based significance test with provable control over false discoveries by deriving a bound on the supremum deviation of quality statistics, reducing resamples by up to 100x while preserving power.
AI.motivation: To address the computational burden of resampling-based significance testing in data mining, especially for large-scale or computationally intensive analyses, while providing rigorous control over false discoveries.
AI.method: Derives a bound on the supremum deviation of the test statistic representing data mining quality and uses it to determine a very small number of resampled datasets needed to guarantee error control, enabling early stopping and scalability across tasks such as pattern mining and network analysis.
AI.result: Achieves up to two orders of magnitude reduction in running time compared with state-of-the-art resampling approaches, with preserved statistical power and applicability to large real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: FewRS offers a general, scalable framework for statistically validating data mining results with rigorous false-discovery guarantees, applicable across diverse analytics tasks where resampling is used.

title: Automated Mediator for Human Negotiation: Pre-Mediation via a Structured LLM Pipeline
summary: Pre-mediation, the preparatory phase preceding direct human negotiation, plays a critical role in achieving mutually beneficial agreements, yet is often omitted due to cost, time, and limited access to trained mediators. We introduce an automated mediator for human negotiation, implemented as a structured pipeline of LLM modules, that supports pre-mediation in integrative negotiation settings. The pipeline decomposes preparation into specialized modules for dialogue, preference prediction, response-level critique, and structured summarization, separating inference, generation, and evaluation to address limitations of monolithic single-prompt approaches. We use the term "agent" for each module following common LLM-systems terminology, but the components are not autonomous and do not interact peer-to-peer; outputs are passed forward in a fixed sequence. We evaluate the system in two controlled human-subject experiments comparing AI-based pre-mediation with professional human mediators in a multi-issue negotiation scenario. On short-term self-reported measures, the automated mediator achieves preparation outcomes broadly comparable to human mediators, including trust in the mediator and confidence in reaching mutually beneficial agreements, while achieving substantially lower error on the preference-inference task under our scenario and prompts (36% lower RMSE). A second study shows that targeted prompt refinements reduce excessive affirmation patterns from 36.6% to 16.8%, matching human mediator baselines. Our findings suggest that structured LLM pipelines can provide scalable, low-effort pre-mediation support broadly comparable to human mediators on short-term self-reported preparation outcomes. The pipeline's single-party design mirrors how human mediators run pre-mediation today and enables parallel deployment across all parties to a dispute, supporting scalability.

AI.tldr: A modular LLM-based mediator pipeline for pre-mediation in integrative negotiations. In two controlled human-subject experiments, it performs comparably to human mediators on short-term prep outcomes, with improved preference-inference accuracy (36% lower RMSE) and reduced overly affirming responses via targeted prompts; its single-party design enables scalable parallel deployment.
AI.motivation: Pre-mediation improves negotiation outcomes but is often skipped due to cost, time, and limited access to trained mediators. A scalable, automated, modular LLM pipeline could provide low-effort pre-mediation support across parties.
AI.method: A fixed-sequence pipeline of LLM-based modules (agents) for dialogue, preference prediction, response-level critique, and structured summarization. The design separates inference, generation, and evaluation to avoid monolithic prompts. Outputs flow forward in a controlled order. Evaluation used two controlled human-subject experiments comparing AI-based pre-mediation with professional mediators in a multi-issue negotiation scenario.
AI.result: The automated mediator achieved short-term preparation outcomes broadly comparable to human mediators on trust and confidence. It showed 36% lower RMSE in the preference-inference task. Targeted prompt refinements reduced excessive affirmation from 36.6% to 16.8%, aligning with human baselines.
AI.conclusion: Structured modular LLM pipelines can provide scalable, low-effort pre-mediation support that is broadly comparable to human mediators for short-term outcomes. The single-party design enables parallel deployment across all dispute parties, enhancing scalability.

title: INFRAMIND: Infrastructure-Aware Multi-Agent Orchestration
summary: Existing multi-agent LLM orchestration methods, ranging from brute-force ensembles to learned routers, select models and topologies based on task and model features. However, these methods do not consider the runtime state of the serving infrastructure. On shared GPU clusters under concurrent load, this infrastructure blindness causes systematic resource underutilization: preferred models accumulate deep request queues while equally capable alternatives sit idle. In multi-agent pipelines, where each query triggers multiple sequential model calls, these delays then compound across every downstream step. Closing this gap is challenging because the relevant infrastructure signals (queue depths, KV-cache pressure, latencies) are dynamic and noisy, and they must drive three different decisions: planning, per-step routing, and scheduling. We introduce INFRAMIND, a framework that makes the entire multi-agent stack infrastructure-aware. An infra-aware planner conditions topology and role selection on real-time system load and remaining budget, biasing toward simpler graphs under congestion and richer ones at low load. An infra-aware executor then observes per-model queue depths, cache utilization, and response latencies at each agent step to decide which model to call and how deeply to reason; a budget-aware scheduler further reorders each model's queue so that urgent requests are served first. Cast as a hierarchical constrained MDP and solved end-to-end via reinforcement learning, the system learns to balance quality against latency automatically. Across five benchmarks, INFRAMIND delivers up to +7.6 pp accuracy over the prior baseline at low load with up to 7x lower latency, and sustains up to 99.9% SLO compliance under high load where every baseline drops below 50%.

AI.tldr: INFRAMIND is an infra-aware reinforcement-learning framework for multi-agent LLM orchestration, jointly optimizing topology, routing, and scheduling based on real-time infrastructure signals to improve latency and accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing infrastructure-blind orchestration leaves GPUs underutilized and causes cascading delays in multi-step pipelines under concurrent load; there is a need to incorporate dynamic system state into decision making.
AI.method: Three components: infra-aware planner (dynamically chooses topology/roles based on system load and budget), infra-aware executor (per-step model selection considering queue depths, KV-cache pressure, response latencies), budget-aware scheduler (reorders model queues to favor urgent requests). Formulated as hierarchical constrained MDP and trained end-to-end with reinforcement learning.
AI.result: Across five benchmarks, achieves up to 7.6 percentage points accuracy gain at low load with up to 7x latency reduction; maintains up to 99.9% SLO compliance under high load where baselines fall below 50%.
AI.conclusion: Infra-aware end-to-end RL approach balances quality vs latency automatically, enabling more efficient, reliable multi-agent LLM orchestration in shared infrastructure.

title: Learning Object Manipulation from Scratch via Contrastive Interaction
summary: Contrastive Reinforcement Learning (CRL) has seen recent success in a wide variety of goal-conditioned robotics tasks by learning structured representations of the dynamics. However, despite its success in locomotion and simpler control domains, CRL often struggles in interaction-rich manipulation. We argue that a key source of this difficulty is object-centric interaction, such as contact or grasping, that induces distinct changes in the underlying dynamic modes. In this work, we formulate manipulation dynamics as a piecewise-smooth Markov process and show that interaction-induced mode changes create piecewise nonlinear reachability structures that are difficult for standard CRL energy functions to represent and plan over. Based on this analysis, we introduce Interaction-weighted Resampling (IWR). IWR performs interaction-aware resampling around phases before, during, and after interactions, encouraging the learned representation to preserve the mode boundaries that determine future reachability to capture multi-modal and piecewise nonlinear reachability. Across interaction-centric environments, including 2D dynamic control, robotic manipulation, and robot air hockey, IWR improves both sample efficiency and overall performance over prior CRL methods, with 19.8% average improvement in simulation. Finally, using a sim-to-real pipeline with policies trained by IWR, we demonstrate the first real-world goal-conditioned robot air hockey agent capable of hitting goals, improving success from 25% to 60%. Project Page: IWR-arxiv.github.io.

AI.tldr: CRL struggles with interaction-rich manipulation due to piecewise nonlinear dynamics; introduces Interaction-weighted Resampling (IWR) to preserve mode boundaries and capture multi-modal reachability, improving sample efficiency and sim-to-real transfer in manipulation and air hockey.
AI.motivation: Standard CRL energy functions struggle to represent interaction-induced mode changes in manipulation, leading to poor planning and generalization in contact-rich tasks.
AI.method: Formulates manipulation dynamics as a piecewise-smooth Markov process and introduces Interaction-weighted Resampling (IWR), which resamples around interaction phases (before/during/after) to preserve mode boundaries and encourage the representation to capture piecewise nonlinear reachability.
AI.result: IWR yields improved sample efficiency and performance across interaction-centric environments (2D dynamic control, robotic manipulation, robot air hockey), with ~19.8% average improvement in simulation; demonstrates sim-to-real transfer, achieving a real-world goal-conditioned robot air hockey agent whose success rises from 25% to 60%.
AI.conclusion: Interaction-aware resampling helps CRL represent and plan over piecewise nonlinear reachability arising from contact, improving performance in manipulation tasks and enabling effective sim-to-real transfer.

title: TRON: Tracing Rays to Orchestrate a Neural Renderer for 3D Gaussian Reconstructions
summary: We introduce TRON, a rendering framework that combines 3D Gaussian ray tracing with neural rendering to enable realistic and controllable rendering of real-world 3D scenes under novel lighting, dynamic object motion, object insertion, and material editing. Prior approaches that rely solely on physically based rendering (PBR) of Gaussian representations struggle to achieve realistic relighting due to imperfections in reconstructed geometry, material estimates, and light transport estimation. At the same time, neural rendering methods often lack an explicit scene representation, limiting their ability to support interactive editing with fine-grained manipulation. TRON bridges these two paradigms. We use intrinsic decomposition priors from a learned inverse rendering model to regularize the material properties of a Gaussian field, and repurpose a ray tracer to provide radiometric guidance rather than final pixels. By treating this output as a structured 3D scaffold, we empower a lightweight neural renderer to bridge the domain gap between shading-model constrained estimates and photorealistic output. Our key insight is that the combination of explicit 3D knowledge with robust material priors provides speed and controllability, while neural rendering enables the synthesis of photorealistic images. To support real-world scenarios, we train our neural renderer with a multi-stage strategy consisting of large-scale pretraining and targeted fine-tuning on a newly constructed dataset of 2.1M rendered synthetic and real-world frames from 3D reconstructions. TRON outperforms Gaussian-based relighting methods in realism, and prior neural renderers in editability and speed. To the best of our knowledge, TRON is the first method to enable practical interactive applications in captured 3D environments, offering realistic appearance under dynamic geometric, lighting and material conditions.

AI.tldr: TRON is a hybrid rendering framework that unites explicit 3D Gaussian ray tracing with neural rendering. It uses intrinsic decomposition priors to regularize material properties and treats the ray-traced radiometric output as a scaffold for a lightweight neural renderer, enabling realistic relighting, dynamic scenes, object editing, and material changes with improved speed and editability. Trained with large-scale pretraining and fine-tuned on 2.1M frames from 3D reconstructions, it outperforms Gaussian-based relighting and prior neural renderers in realism and interactivity.
AI.motivation: Overcoming two main limitations: (1) Gaussian/PBR-based relighting struggles due to imperfect geometry, materials, and light transport estimation; (2) Neural rendering lacks explicit scene representations, hindering fine-grained interactive editing. A hybrid approach can deliver both realism and controllability for captured 3D environments.
AI.method: Represent scenes as explicit 3D Gaussian fields; use a ray tracer to obtain radiometric guidance rather than final pixels; regularize material properties with intrinsic decomposition priors from a learned inverse rendering model; employ a lightweight neural renderer to translate the 3D scaffold into photorealistic output; train via large-scale pretraining plus targeted fine-tuning on a dataset of 2.1M synthetic and real-world frames from 3D reconstructions.
AI.result: TRON achieves higher realism than Gaussian-based relighting methods and better editability and speed than prior neural renderers.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates a practical pipeline that leverages explicit 3D geometry and robust material priors to enable realistic, editable, and fast rendering in real-world captured environments, including dynamic geometry and lighting changes.

title: Search Discipline for Long-Horizon Research Agents
summary:
Autoresearch agents now propose, evaluate, and select scientific candidates against a metric, and that metric is usually an aggregate reduced over a heterogeneous space of regions, slices, or cohorts. We show that when scientific validity lives in that disaggregated structure, the aggregate can rank the wrong candidate first. The headline number improves while the structure underneath inverts, so a decision made on the number accepts a candidate that quietly breaks the model. The failure is not domain-specific. It appears wherever a candidate's validity is multi-dimensional but its verifier is a single reduction.
  We demonstrate the inversion on a fire-model task in the Ecosystem Demography model. The highest-scoring candidate and a slightly lower one are within noise of each other on global score, yet the top-scoring one collapses the protected boreal regions while the other preserves them. What separates them is the per-region behavior, not the headline number.
  This decision should not be left to the agent that produced the candidates. The agent optimizing the score is the last party likely to catch the score being wrong, and a prompt has no remaining turn once the agent has stopped. We move the decision to an external control loop that audits each candidate on its disaggregated behavior and acts after the agent has decided. It can demote a candidate the agent would have accepted, and it can reopen a run the agent had declared finished. Our contribution is the inversion finding itself, and a search-discipline protocol that decides on reviewable candidate-effect evidence instead of the score.

AI.tldr: Aggregated performance metrics can misrank candidates when validity is multi-dimensional; an external audit loop on disaggregated regional effects is needed to avoid decisions that favor headline scores over critical regional impacts.
AI.motivation: Mitigate inversion risk where a candidate with the best aggregate score causes meaningful harm in subgroups/regions due to multi-dimensional validity that is collapsed by a single metric.
AI.method: Demonstrate inversion on a fire-model task within the Ecosystem Demography model, compare near-equal global scores but divergent per-region outcomes, and propose a protocol for an external control loop to reassess and possibly demote or reopen candidate runs based on disaggregated evidence.
AI.result: Showed that the top-scoring candidate can collapse protected boreal regions while a slightly lower-scoring alternative preserves them; this reveals that the global score hides critical regional failures; proposed a search-discipline protocol for reviewable evidence rather than relying on the single score.
AI.conclusion: Decision-making should involve an external audit of disaggregated candidate effects; the inversion finding calls for a controlled loop that can demote, demote, or reopen runs based on disaggregated validity rather than the headline metric.

title: Bernstein-Schur Kernels: Random Features by Sketched Modulation and Radial Randomization
summary: Bernstein--Schur kernels are products of a finite-feature kernel (one with an explicit finite-dimensional feature map) and a completely monotone shift-invariant kernel: nonstationary kernels that fall between the shift-invariant and dot-product templates random features usually exploit, so in general neither Bochner sampling nor polynomial sketching applies to the full kernel directly. We give one random-feature construction for the whole class that \emph{randomizes both factors: it sketches the finite modulation and randomizes the completely monotone radial factor, sampling the latter's one-dimensional Bernstein--Widder scale and then applying Gaussian random Fourier features (whose frequency is still $d$-dimensional). The feature dimension is then $Dm$, set by the sketch size $m$ and the radial-draw count $D$, free of the $O(d^2)$ size of the exact modulation feature. Keeping the modulation \emph{exact} is the analyzable limit ($m\to\infty$): there we prove unbiasedness, an exact variance for the recommended flat estimator, an expected matrix-Bernstein operator-norm bound (with a matching high-probability tail) controlled by the top eigenvalues of the kernel and modulation Gram matrices together with an intrinsic dimension rather than the crude $N\max_{ij}$ entrywise route, and a deterministic relative-spectral kernel-ridge stability result. By conditioning on the sketch, the doubly-randomized estimator inherits the same intrinsic-dimension operator-norm guarantee plus a single additive sketch term, tunable by $m$ independently of $D$. The motivating instance is the biased $yat$-kernel $k_{yat,b}(w,x)=(w^\top x+b)^2/(\|w-x\|^2+\varepsilon)$, $b\ge0$, whose family span contains the inverse-multiquadric kernel by finite differences in $b$; for it the radial mixture is the IMQ spectral sampler, and one frequency per scale is variance-optimal at a fixed radial-feature budget.

AI.tldr: A doubly-randomized feature map for Bernstein–Schur nonstationary kernels: sketch finite modulation and randomize the completely monotone radial factor, producing a feature dimension Dm with provable unbiasedness and operator-norm guarantees; analyzed in the m→∞ limit; illustrated on a biased yat,b kernel with IMQ radial sampler.
AI.motivation: Nonstationary kernels bridging shift-invariant and dot-product templates are not amenable to standard Bochner sampling or polynomial sketches; need scalable, provably reliable random-feature approximations that capture both the modulation and radial components.
AI.method: Sketch the finite modulation with a random feature budget m and sample the Bernstein–Widder scale for the radial, completely monotone factor; couple with Gaussian random Fourier features in d dimensions for the frequency; total feature dimension is Dm; analyze both the full-randomization (m finite) and the modulation-exact limit (m→∞) with conditioning on the sketch; derive intrinsic-dimension operator-norm bounds and tails; provide a stability guarantee for kernel ridge in spectral form.
AI.result: In the limit m→∞, the estimator is unbiased with a computable variance for the flat estimator and an explicit operator-norm bound (high-probability tail) controlled by the top eigenvalues of the kernel and modulation Gram matrices and the intrinsic dimension; conditioning on the sketch preserves these guarantees with an additive sketch term tunable by m, independent of D.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a practical, provably reliable random-feature construction for the full Bernstein–Schur class, avoiding the O(d^2) size of exact modulation features by sketching, while maintaining strong spectral guarantees; the yat,b kernel example with IMQ radial sampling exemplifies the approach and indicates variance-optimality with one scale-frequency per budget.

title: NSVQ: Mitigating Codebook Collapse by Stabilizing Encoder Drift in Vector Quantization
summary: Vector quantization is central to modern generative modeling pipelines, but large-codebook VQ models often suffer from codebook collapse. We identify encoder drift as a key driver of this failure: as the encoder moves the latent distribution, sparsely updated code vectors can lag behind, lose assignments, and increase quantization error, creating a feedback loop through the straight-through estimator. We propose NSVQ, a non-stationary-aware VQ training strategy that combines a dense non-stationary embedding loss, codebook replacement, and stage-wise encoder freezing. NSVQ first helps the codebook track encoder drift during early training, then freezes the encoder to consolidate the codebook under a fixed latent geometry, and finally reintroduces adversarial refinement. Experiments on ImageNet-1k show that NSVQ improves reconstruction quality while maintaining full codebook utilization. On ImageNet-1k at 128$\times$128 with 65,536 codes, NSVQ reduces rFID from 2.39 to 2.10 compared with SimVQ, while both methods maintain 100\% utilization. Additional latent diffusion experiments show that NSVQ also improves downstream ImageNet generation FID.

AI.tldr: NSVQ is a training strategy for large-codebook VQ that mitigates encoder drift and codebook collapse by combining a dense non-stationary embedding loss, codebook replacement, and stage-wise encoder freezing, leading to improved reconstruction and full codebook utilization on ImageNet-1k and better latent-diffusion generation.
AI.motivation: Large-codebook VQ models suffer from encoder drift which desynchronizes the latent distribution and causes sparse updates, codebook lag, and codebook collapse via a feedback loop with the straight-through estimator.
AI.method: A three-stage training protocol: (1) dense non-stationary embedding loss to let the codebook track encoder drift; (2) codebook replacement and stage-wise encoder freezing to consolidate the codebook under a fixed latent geometry; (3) reintroduction of adversarial refinement to fine-tune the system.
AI.result: On ImageNet-1k (128x128) with 65,536 codes, NSVQ reduces rFID from 2.39 to 2.10 relative to SimVQ while maintaining 100% codebook utilization; latent diffusion experiments show improved downstream ImageNet generation FID.
AI.conclusion: NSVQ effectively mitigates encoder drift and codebook collapse in large-codebook VQ, achieving better reconstruction quality and sustained full codebook utilization, with transferable gains to latent-diffusion based generation.

title: HERO: Hindsight-Enhanced Reflection from Environment Observations for Agentic Self-Distillation
summary: Reinforcement learning typically improves multi-turn agent capabilities through the terminal outcome of the trajectories, which makes it difficult to determine credit assignments for each intermediate turns. Recent on-policy self-distillation methods offer a promising alternative by converting privileged feedback into dense token-level supervision through a self-teacher. Our study is motivated by the unexpected performance degradation observed when naively extending this paradigm to multi-turn settings, which we attribute to a lack of alignment between privileged feedback, such as successful trajectories or terminal outcomes, and the student's current decision context. We introduce HERO, a hindsight-enhanced self-distillation framework that uses next environment observations as locally aligned feedback. After each rollout, HERO reflects on the completed interaction to convert each observation into a compact turn-level diagnosis, that captures actionable feedback about the original action such as its necessity, validity or failure cause. On TauBench and WebShop, HERO improves task success and reduces unnecessary turns over environment-feedback-only self-distillation and GRPO. It is especially effective under limited training turn budgets, where successful rollouts are rare and GRPO provides weak reward-contrast signals.

AI.tldr: HERO is a hindsight-enhanced self-distillation framework for multi-turn RL that aligns feedback with local decision contexts by converting observations into turn-level diagnoses after each rollout, leading to better task success and fewer unnecessary turns, especially with limited training turns, outperforming environment-feedback-only self-distillation and GRPO on TauBench and WebShop.
AI.motivation: Credit assignment in multi-turn RL is difficult when using terminal outcomes. Naive extension of on-policy self-distillation fails due to misalignment between privileged feedback (success, terminal reward) and the agent's current decision context.
AI.method: After each rollout, HERO uses the next environment observations to produce a compact turn-level diagnosis that reflects feedback about the original action (necessity, validity, failure cause). This hindsight, locally aligned feedback substitutes or augments privileged feedback to guide learning.
AI.result: HERO improves task success and reduces unnecessary turns compared to environment-feedback-only self-distillation and GRPO on TauBench and WebShop, especially under limited training turn budgets where successful rollouts are rare and GRPO offers weak reward-contrast signals.
AI.conclusion: Aligning privileged feedback with the current decision context through hindsight-derived, turn-level diagnoses yields more informative supervision for multi-turn RL, enhancing performance under data-scarce regimes and broadening applicability over existing self-distillation and reward-based methods.

title: Quantifying Subliminal Behavioral Transfer Ratios in Language Model Distillation
summary: Distillation of a language model intended to transfer benign behavior to a student model may also transfer undesirable characteristics, if they are present in the teacher model, a phenomenon known as subliminal learning. While qualitative evidence supports the existence of this effect, its magnitude has not been systematically characterized. This study quantifies subliminal behavioral transfer ratios by steering two teacher models (Llama-2-7B-Chat and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) at varying steering strengths and distilling student models using only benign data. Evaluation on 100 JailbreakBench prompts with GPT-4.1, serving as the evaluator, indicates that transfer is robust but exhibits distinct scaling behaviors. Llama-2 demonstrates a sharp threshold ($τ= {0.25,0.32} \ \text{beyond} \ α= -0.15$), whereas Qwen2.5 displays continuous and higher levels of transfer ($τ$ up to $0.61$).

AI.tldr: Subliminal learning during distillation transfers undesirable behavior from teacher to student in a model-agnostic way; transfer magnitude is quantified using two teachers across steering strengths, revealing model-dependent scaling: Llama-2 shows a sharp threshold, Qwen2.5 shows continuous transfer up to τ=0.61.
AI.motivation: To quantify the magnitude and scaling of subliminal behavioral transfer during distillation and assess risk implications for safety-aligned student models.
AI.method: Distill student models from two teacher LLMs (Llama-2-7B-Chat and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) using benign data with varying steering strengths. Evaluate guidance transfer using 100 JailbreakBench prompts evaluated by GPT-4.1 as the evaluator; compute transfer ratios and analyze scaling behavior with respect to steering strength parameters (τ, α).
AI.result: Subliminal transfer is robust but scales nonlinearly and model-dependently: Llama-2 exhibits a sharp threshold (τ≈0.25–0.32 beyond α=−0.15), while Qwen2.5 shows continuous transfer with higher magnitude, reaching τ up to 0.61.
AI.conclusion: Subliminal transfer during distillation is not uniform across models and steering settings; its non-linear, model-specific behavior necessitates careful evaluation and mitigation in safety-focused distillation pipelines.

title: Organize then Retrieve: Hierarchical Memory Navigation for Efficient Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents struggle with long-horizon tasks due to their inherent statelessness, requiring all task-relevant information to be encoded in growing input contexts. The resulting degraded reasoning quality, increased inference cost, and higher latency necessitate efficient working memory mechanisms. However, existing approaches either rely on lossy compression or similarity-based retrieval, which often fail to capture temporal structure and causal dependencies required for multi-step agentic tasks. In this work, we present HORMA, a Hierarchical Organize-and-Retrieve Memory Agent that organizes experience into a file-system-like hierarchical structure, where summarized entities are linked to the corresponding raw trajectories, enabling efficient access without losing detailed information. HORMA decomposes working memory into two stages: structured memory construction and navigation-based retrieval. The construction module iteratively refines how experiences are structured by distinguishing between failures caused by missing information and those caused by misleading or overloaded context. The navigation module retrieves task-relevant context by traversing the hierarchy using a lightweight agent trained with reinforcement learning to select minimal yet sufficient context, thereby reducing latency along the critical execution path. Across ALFWorld, LoCoMo, and LongMemEval, HORMA improves task performance under constrained context budgets while requiring at most 22.17% of the baseline token usage in long conversation tasks. Compared to existing methods, it consistently achieves better efficiency-performance trade-offs and generalizes effectively to unseen tasks.

AI.tldr: HORMA introduces a hierarchical organize-and-retrieve memory for LLM agents that links summarized entities to raw trajectories in a file-system-like structure, enabling efficient access and preserving details under tight context budgets.
AI.motivation: LLM agents are effectively stateless; long-horizon tasks require maintaining temporal structure and causal dependencies. Existing lossy compression and similarity-based retrieval fail to capture this structure, leading to degraded reasoning, higher latency, and inefficiency.
AI.method: A two-stage memory system: (1) structured memory construction that iteratively refines how experiences are structured, distinguishing between missing information and misleading/overloaded context; (2) navigation-based retrieval where a lightweight RL agent traverses the hierarchy to select minimal yet sufficient context.
AI.result: HORMA yields improved task performance under constrained context budgets across ALFWorld, LoCoMo, and LongMemEval, using at most 22.17% of baseline tokens in long conversations; it achieves better efficiency-performance trade-offs and generalizes to unseen tasks.
AI.conclusion: HORMA provides an efficient, scalable working memory for long-horizon reasoning by hierarchically organizing experiences and enabling targeted retrieval, reducing latency while preserving essential details.

title: FlowBank: Query-Adaptive Agentic Workflows Optimization through Precompute-and-Reuse
summary: Large Language Model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems are increasingly powerful, but current agentic workflow optimization paradigms make an unsatisfying trade-off. Task-level methods spend substantial offline compute yet deploy only a single workflow, leaving complementary candidates unused, while query-level methods synthesize a new workflow per query at substantial inference cost. Our motivating analysis shows these paradigms are more complementary than competing: workflows discovered during offline search often solve different subsets of queries, and many queries handled by expensive query-level generation can already be solved by cheaper precomputed workflows. This suggests a different objective: rather than searching for one universally best workflow or regenerating one per instance, we should build a compact bank of reusable, complementary workflows and select among them adaptively at inference time. Doing so requires solving three coupled problems: generating complementary rather than redundant candidates, compressing them into a small deployable portfolio, and assigning each query to the right workflow under a performance-cost trade-off. To this end, we present FlowBank, a three-stage framework for portfolio-based agentic workflow optimization. Diversifying proposes DiverseFlow to steer search toward under-covered queries and produce a high-coverage candidate pool. Curating proposes CuraFlow to compress this pool into a compact portfolio with minimal redundancy. Matching casts deployment as edge-value prediction on a query-workflow bipartite graph and routes each incoming query to the portfolio member with the best predicted utility. Across five benchmarks, FlowBank achieves the highest average score among the evaluated methods while remaining cost-competitive, improving over the strongest automated and handcrafted baselines by 4.26% and 14.92% relative, respectively.

AI.tldr: FlowBank introduces a portfolio-based, three-stage framework (DiverseFlow, CuraFlow, and Matching) to build and deploy a compact set of complementary, reusable workflows for LLM-based agentic tasks, achieving higher performance with competitive cost across five benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current regimes either optimize a single offline-generated workflow or regenerate a new workflow per query, leading to inefficiencies and missed opportunities where precomputed workflows suffice. The abstract argues that discovering complementary workflows and routing queries to them can outperform single-best or per-query approaches by exploiting diversity and reducing runtime cost.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) Diversifying: DiverseFlow searches to produce a high-coverage pool of candidate workflows focusing on under-covered queries; (2) Curating: CuraFlow compresses this pool into a small, non-redundant portfolio; (3) Matching: Deploys via edge-value prediction on a bipartite graph, routing each query to the workflow with the best predicted utility under a performance-cost trade-off.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across five benchmarks shows FlowBank achieves the highest average score among evaluated methods and is cost-competitive, with relative improvements of 4.26% over the strongest automated baselines and 14.92% over handcrafted baselines.
AI.conclusion: Composite, portfolio-based optimization that emphasizes complementary coverage and compact deployment can outperform single workflows or per-query generation. The key contributions are framing workflow optimization as portfolio design, explicit diversification to cover query space, and learned routing to balance performance and cost.

title: 4DP-QA: Scalable QA for 4D Perception in Vision Language Models
summary: Despite recent advances, Vision Language Models (VLMs) still struggle to grasp the dynamics of the world. We note that the ability to reason about a 4D scene, challenging in itself, is further complicated by two factors. First, VLMs observe motion indirectly via its projection onto 2D images. Second, existing datasets fail to disentangle object and camera motion. To address these challenges, we present a QA generation pipeline that focuses on motion-related scene understanding. We take particular care of the entanglement of camera and object motion by casting tracking in both the traditional way and in a novel, fixed reference system, dubbed True-Motion Tracking, which provides an intuitive description of motion. From this pipeline, we generate a large-scale training dataset of 400K samples, 4DP-QA (4D Perception QA), and a 2.2K-sample benchmark, 4DP-QA-Bench. Training existing models on our dataset yields performance improvements on an external benchmark, validating the effectiveness of our method.

AI.tldr: Proposes a large-scale 4D motion QA dataset (4DP-QA) and a motion-focused benchmark (4DP-QA-Bench) for vision-language models, using a novel True-Motion Tracking to disentangle camera and object motion; demonstrates improved performance on external benchmarks after training on the dataset.
AI.motivation: VLMs have limited 4D scene understanding because motion is observed only through 2D projections and camera/object motion are entangled. A dedicated motion-centric QA dataset and evaluation is needed to train and assess 4D reasoning.
AI.method: A QA generation pipeline centered on motion understanding. It uses traditional tracking and a novel fixed-reference True-Motion Tracking to provide intuitive motion descriptions that disentangle camera from object motion. Generates 400K samples for 4DP-QA and a 2.2K-sample benchmark 4DP-QA-Bench. Models trained on this data show improved performance on an external benchmark.
AI.result: Training on 4DP-QA yields measurable gains on an external benchmark for motion reasoning tasks, validating the dataset and approach.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dataset and True-Motion Tracking framework effectively advance 4D scene understanding in VLMs by disentangling camera and object motion and providing scalable training and evaluation resources that improve external benchmarks.

title: Recursive Binding on a Budget: Subspace Carving in Order-p Tensor Memories
summary: Tensor Product Representations provide the structural fidelity required for symbolic reasoning in models but suffer from exponential dimensionality growth when encoding deep recursive structures. Conversely, Vector Symbolic Architectures maintain constant dimensionality but sacrifice capacity and fidelity due to noisy compression via superposition. In this work, we propose Orthogonal Subspace Carving (OSC), a memory architecture that binds fillers to roles by projecting onto the null space of the role basis before aggregating into a fixed order-p tensor. OSC uses projections to enforce geometric orthogonality between bound structures within a static memory trace. We show that this mechanism decouples the tensor order from the structural depth, enabling deep recursive binding within a constant memory footprint. By performing retrieval via recognition, this construction allows for component vectors that are orders of magnitude smaller than the memory tensor, giving superior memory efficiency in settings involving high superposition. We also show that TPR is a special case of binding in Clifford algebra, and give a Clifford formulation of OSC.

AI.tldr: OSC binds fillers to roles via null-space projections, enabling deep recursive binding with constant memory and improved efficiency under high superposition; it has a Clifford-algebra formulation, and TPR is a Clifford-binding special case.
AI.motivation: To overcome exponential memory growth in tensor product representations (TPR) for deep recursive structures, while maintaining the efficiency of vector symbolic architectures (VSAs).
AI.method: Bind fillers to roles by projecting onto the null space of the role basis, then aggregate into a fixed order-p tensor, enforcing geometric orthogonality within a static memory trace. Retrieval is performed by recognition. The approach decouples tensor order from depth and yields a Clifford-algebra formulation (OSC).
AI.result: Decouples tensor order from structural depth, enabling deep recursive binding in constant memory. Retrieval uses component vectors that are much smaller than the memory tensor, improving memory efficiency in high superposition. TPR is shown to be a special case of Clifford-algebra binding; OSC has a Clifford formulation.
AI.conclusion: OSC provides a memory architecture that combines high structural fidelity with fixed memory footprint, bridging TPR and VSA approaches through a Clifford-algebra perspective.

title: The Art of Interrogation: Consistency Amplifies Factuality in Spatial Reasoning
summary: Current Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) exhibit remarkable general capabilities but significantly underperform in spatial reasoning tasks. Existing approaches treat this gap as a knowledge deficit, relying on supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to ingest labeled spatial data from external vision sources or synthetic engines. In contrast, we argue that for many tasks, spatial reasoning capabilities are already present in pre-trained LRMs but require alignment through logical coherence under geometric 2D and 3D constraints. In this work, we propose a self-supervised reinforcement learning (RL) framework that targets the internal reasoning process without requiring ground-truth annotations. By formalizing the notion of consistency verifiers -- reward functions that check for geometric and semantic consistency under transformations -- we demonstrate that models can improve their spatial reasoning abilities. We use both image transformations, like flipping, and textual transformations, like swapping the order of objects in the question, and propose a new optimal transport-based RL strategy, OT-GRPO, which is a minimal-matching variant of group relative policy optimization tailored to pairwise verifiers. We show that this label-free consistency training approaches the accuracy of models trained with ground-truth supervision and achieves similar generalization across diverse tasks and data domains.

AI.tldr: Self-supervised consistency reinforcement learning (via OT-GRPO) trains large reasoning models to perform spatial reasoning without ground-truth labels by using geometric/semantic verifiers and transformation-based rewards.
AI.motivation: LRMs underperform in spatial reasoning; existing work relies on supervised fine-tuning with external data. The paper argues that alignment of internal reasoning under geometric constraints can unlock spatial capabilities without labeled data.
AI.method: Introduce consistency verifiers as reward signals checking geometric/semantic consistency under transformations (e.g., image flips, textual object-order swaps). Propose OT-GRPO, an optimal-transport-based minimal-matching variant of group relative policy optimization for pairwise verifiers, enabling label-free RL.
AI.result: Label-free consistency training approaches the accuracy of supervised models and generalizes across diverse tasks and data domains, narrowing the gap in spatial reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Alignment through logical coherence under 2D/3D constraints, via self-supervised RL with verifiers, can unlock robust spatial reasoning in LRMs without ground-truth supervision.

title: AVIS: Adaptive Test-Time Scaling for Vision-Language Models
summary: Modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs) benefit from chain-of-thought prompting and test-time scaling, but these gains often come with prohibitive inference cost due to large visual contexts and long decoding chains. We view this cost through two coupled axes: Visual Context Scaling (VCS), which controls how much visual evidence is passed to the language model, and Visual Reasoning Scaling (VRS), which controls how much inference-time reasoning search is performed. Existing methods typically optimize one axis at a time, leaving the joint allocation of compute across these axes underexplored. We introduce Adaptive Visual Inference Scaling (AVIS), a lightweight policy that adapts both VCS and VRS per query. AVIS realizes VCS through Key Diversity Visual (KDV) pruning, a training-free $O(N)$ key-based rule for removing redundant visual tokens before prefilling, and realizes VRS through adaptive self-consistency, using a learned difficulty predictor to select the number of reasoning rollouts. AVIS is deployment-friendly and compatible with shared-prefill inference, where all rollouts reuse a single prefilling pass and KV cache. Across diverse image and video reasoning benchmarks, AVIS improves the accuracy--compute trade-off relative to VCS-only and VRS-only baselines, and remains effective on top of RL post-trained VLMs while keeping compute and latency low.

AI.tldr: A joint adaptive inference framework AVIS for Vision-Language Models that simultaneously scales visual context (VCS) and reasoning search (VRS) using KDV pruning and adaptive self-consistency, yielding better accuracy-compute trade-offs and compatibility with shared-prefill inference.
AI.motivation: Large VLMs incur high inference cost from processing abundant visual tokens and long chain-of-thought reasoning; existing work typically optimizes VCS or VRS independently; need joint allocation to improve efficiency without sacrificing accuracy; aim for deployment-friendly methods.
AI.method: Adaptive Visual Inference Scaling (AVIS) policy that selects per query VCS and VRS. VCS via Key Diversity Visual (KDV) pruning: a training-free O(N) rule to prune redundant visual tokens before prefilling. VRS via adaptive self-consistency: a learned difficulty predictor to determine the number of reasoning rollouts. Supports shared-prefill inference with a single prefilling pass and KV cache, enabling deployment efficiency.
AI.result: Empirical gains on image and video reasoning benchmarks: improved accuracy-compute trade-offs vs baselines optimizing only VCS or VRS; robust to RL post-trained VLMs; maintains low compute and latency.
AI.conclusion: Joint adaptive scaling is beneficial for VLM efficiency and accuracy; AVIS is deployment-friendly; potential extension to broader settings and multi-modal tasks; future work could refine difficulty predictor or apply to other architectures.

title: Point Cloud Segmentation for Autonomous Clip Positioning in Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy on a Phantom
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures, accepted to IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RAL)
summary: High-risk applications in robotics, such as robot-assisted surgery, present unique challenges. These systems must be both highly precise and interpretable in order to be deployed in environments with very low tolerance for error or unsafe exploration. We present the first robotic system to demonstrate autonomous clip positioning on a physical phantom in laparoscopic surgery, one of the most common interventions in general surgery. After segmentation of a colorless point cloud from a single camera, target positions for the clips are extracted using spline interpolation, and can then be adjusted by the human operator. The segmentation model is trained on only 60 hand-labeled real point clouds, reflecting data scarcity in the surgical domain. We overcome this with a combination of pre-training on 128,000 synthetic point clouds and two novel data augmentation techniques. The motion of the end-effector to each target is visualized for the operator, satisfying the unique motion constraints of minimally-invasive surgery while ensuring that the robot's actions are verifiable and interpretable. In real robot experiments, our system localizes targets with the required precision of 0.75mm at a 95% success rate and executes autonomous clip positioning with a 100% success rate. We provide insights that are applicable to many other surgical and non-surgical tasks that require identifying and navigating to a precise target. Source code and project page: https://github.com/balazsgyenes/kirurc

AI.tldr: Autonomous robotic clip positioning in laparoscopic surgery using single-camera colorless point-cloud segmentation, spline-based target extraction, and data-efficient learning with synthetic pretraining; achieves 0.75 mm precision at 95% success and 100% autonomous positioning on a physical phantom.
AI.motivation: High-risk robotic applications require high precision and interpretability; in surgery data are scarce, yet verifiable action is essential. The work aims to enable accurate, interpretable autonomous targeting under data constraints by leveraging synthetic data, augmentation, and human-in-the-loop oversight.
AI.method: 1) Segment a colorless point cloud from a single camera to locate targets. 2) Extract target positions via spline interpolation; allow human operator adjustments. 3) Pre-train on 128,000 synthetic point clouds and apply two novel data augmentation techniques to mitigate real-data scarcity. 4) Visualize end-effector motion to satisfy surgical motion constraints and support verifiability. 5) Validate in real robot experiments for autonomous clip positioning.
AI.result: Target localization precision of 0.75 mm at 95% success; autonomous clip positioning achieved with 100% success in real robot experiments; demonstration on a physical phantom; system supports interpretable, verifiable actions and offers generalizable insights for other precise targeting tasks.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that data-efficient, interpretable autonomy can be achieved in challenging surgical settings by combining synthetic pretraining, targeted data augmentation, and human-in-the-loop target refinement. The methodology and insights are applicable to a wide range of surgical and non-surgical tasks requiring precise targeting and verifiable robot actions.

title: MODF-SIR: A Multi-agent Omni-modal Distilled Framework for Social Intelligence Reasoning
summary: We propose a multi-agent collaborative framework built upon a lightweight Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM), specifically designed for social intelligence reasoning. A key feature of our approach is that both the training and inference phases are augmented via knowledge distillation. Within this architecture, multi-modal data pertinent to social intelligence is precisely localized. Furthermore, relevant long-tail events are identified, extracted, and rendered as formatted, explicit text. This formatting strategy prevents critical long-tail information from being overshadowed by head events and environmental noise during the tokenization process. Specifically, we integrate Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) across the entire reasoning pipeline, encompassing the extraction and representation of long-tail events, Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting, and self-reflection. This TTA mechanism is also distillation-enhanced, utilizing Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to fine-tune the foundation model exclusively for instance-level reasoning. Extensive evaluations against various open-source and proprietary AI models across multiple benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework. With around 30% of training data from IntentTrain, we achieve state-of-the-art results. Codes are available at https://github.com/eeee-sys/MODF-SIR, demo is available at https://huggingface.co/spaces/Harry-1234/MODF-SIR, LoRA is available at https://huggingface.co/Harry-1234/MODF-SIR and the dataset for training router is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/Harry-1234/IntentRouterTrain.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent framework using a lightweight multimodal LLM for social intelligence, enhanced by training and inference distillation, explicit long-tail event handling, and test-time adaptation with LoRA, achieving state-of-the-art results with ~30% IntentTrain data; code and demos available online.
AI.motivation: Address limitations in social-intelligence reasoning for multimodal inputs, such as robust long-tail event handling, efficient training/inference, and coherent reasoning under uncertainty; leverage knowledge distillation and test-time adaptation to improve performance with limited data.
AI.method: A multi-agent collaborative framework built on a lightweight multimodal LLM, with knowledge distillation guiding both training and inference; precise localization of multimodal cues; extraction and formatting of long-tail events as explicit text to preserve information; end-to-end Test-Time Adaptation across extraction, representation, CoT prompting, and self-reflection, all augmented via distillation and fine-tuned with LoRA for instance-level reasoning.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations against open-source and proprietary models on multiple benchmarks show strong performance, achieving state-of-the-art results with around 30% of training data from IntentTrain; publicly available code, demos, LoRA weights, and training data are provided.
AI.conclusion: Integrating distillation-based training/inference, explicit long-tail-event formatting, and comprehensive test-time adaptation in a lightweight MLLM framework yields robust social-intelligence reasoning and strong data-efficiency, with practical reproducibility.

title: Contactless 3D Human Body Measurement Using Depth Cameras for Smart Health Monitoring
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures. Depth camera-based framework for contactless anthropometric measurement and geometric analysis using 3D point clouds
summary: Contactless body measurement technologies are becoming increasingly significant for smart health monitoring, digital health applications, and remote patient assessment. Traditional anthropometric measurements typically necessitate physical contact and trained personnel, which may constrain scalability in remote healthcare settings. In this study, we introduce a depth camera-based framework for estimating human body measurements utilizing 3D point cloud data. An Orbbec Astra 2 depth camera was employed to capture RGB images, depth maps, and 3D point clouds of participants. The captured point cloud was processed using Python-based tools, including Open3D, NumPy, and OpenCV, to segment the human body from the background. Key anthropometric measurements, such as height and arm span, were computed. The measurements were obtained through a combination of spatial filtering and landmark selection on the 3D point cloud, followed by the projection of the computed measurements onto the corresponding RGB image using camera intrinsic parameters. In addition to linear measurements, the approximate body volume and visible surface area were estimated using voxel-based occupancy analysis and mesh-based surface reconstruction methods. The experimental results from a single depth capture demonstrated that accurate body measurements and geometric estimates could be obtained from depth camera data without physical contact. This study provides a foundation for future real-time systems that integrate depth sensing with intelligent health monitoring and generative AI models for smart healthcare applications.

AI.tldr: A non-contact, depth-camera–based method estimates anthropometric metrics (height, arm span) from 3D point clouds; also approximates body volume and surface area using voxel occupancy and mesh reconstruction, enabling scalable remote health monitoring.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable, contactless body measurements in remote healthcare settings where traditional anthropometrics require in-person measurements and trained personnel.
AI.method: Capture RGB, depth, and 3D point clouds with an Orbbec Astra 2 depth camera. Segment the human body from the background using Python tools (Open3D, NumPy, OpenCV). Compute linear measurements via spatial filtering and landmark localization on the 3D point cloud, then project measurements onto the RGB image using camera intrinsics. Estimate body volume via voxel-based occupancy analysis and surface area via mesh-based reconstruction.
AI.result: Demonstrated that accurate body measurements and geometric estimates can be obtained from a single depth capture without physical contact, indicating feasibility for non-contact health monitoring and paving the way for real-time systems using depth sensing and AI.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a feasible, non-contact approach for obtaining key anthropometrics and geometric properties from depth data, serving as a foundation for real-time smart healthcare applications and integration with generative AI models.

title: Signed Compression Progress on a Sealed Audit is Goodhart-Resistant
comment: 16 pages, 7 figures. Lean 4 (Mathlib) mechanized core and ARC-TGI experiment code: https://github.com/Zetetic-Dhruv/audit-compression-progress
summary:
Compression progress is a long-standing proposal for intrinsic motivation: reward an agent when its world model becomes better at predicting or compressing experience. The folk claim is that this reward is "credible" because it is paid only for learning. We make this precise and prove it. If intrinsic reward is the signed decrease of a fixed sealed-audit loss, r_t = E(theta_{t-1}) - E(theta_t), then cumulative reward telescopes exactly to endpoint audit improvement, so no policy can push reward up indefinitely while true audit performance stagnates or degrades. For finite audit panels the same result holds with a sharp false-positive budget: cumulative empirical reward is at most true audit improvement plus 2 Delta_n(F, delta), the uniform audit deviation of the model class. This is horizon-free: adaptivity over time costs nothing once the sealed panel uniformly controls the class.
  The theorem also identifies the failure modes: the guarantee disappears if progress is clipped, scored on the agent's own stream, exposed to a high-capacity model on a reusable panel, or applied to a neural class that makes Delta_n vacuous. We give a Lean 4 mechanization of the structural core (telescoping, the finite-audit bound, finite Gibbs, and the entropy floor) and an experiment suite on ARC-TGI grid-transformation generators with adaptive holdout attacks. Experiments confirm the theory: finite-audit deviation scales as n^{-0.527}; signed progress resists clip-farming, stream leakage, and noisy-TV curiosity; naive reusable audits are exploitable by black-box scalar feedback, while standard release defenses keep the attack below the 2 Delta_n threshold. Signed compression progress on a sealed audit is an accounting signal of genuine improvement.

AI.tldr: Intrinsic reward r_t defined as the negative change in a fixed audit loss E(θ) across training steps yields a telescoping cumulative reward equal to true audit improvement; finite-audit bounds cap cumulative reward with a function of audit deviation; horizon-free; identification of failure modes; Lean 4 formalization and experiments validate theory.
AI.motivation: To formalize compression progress as a credible intrinsic motivation by proving that the reward is faithful to genuine model-audit improvement and resistant to reward hacking under sealed audits; to quantify limits via finite-audit deviations and to provide formal and empirical validation.
AI.method: Define r_t = E(θ_{t-1}) - E(θ_t) with sealed-audit loss E; prove telescoping; derive finite-audit bound using uniform deviation Δ_n; establish horizon-free property; identify failure modes; provide Lean 4 mechanization; run experiments on ARC-TGI grid-transformations with adaptive holdout attacks.
AI.result: Cumulative reward equals endpoint audit improvement; finite-audit deviation yields an upper bound; adaptivity costs nothing under sealed audit; experiments show finite-audit deviation scales as n^{-0.527}; compression progress resists certain attacks; reusable audits expose vulnerabilities; release defenses keep attack under 2Δ_n.
AI.conclusion: Signed compression progress on a sealed audit serves as a credible accounting signal of genuine improvement; the framework exposes when the guarantee fails and provides a mechanized core; practical implications hinge on enforcing sealed audits and controlling model class capacity.

title: Human-Enhanced Loop Modeling (HELM): Agent-Based Finite Element Modeling of Concrete Bridge Barriers
summary: Finite element (FE) modeling of safety-critical infrastructure such as bridge barriers requires high-fidelity nonlinear dynamic analysis, yet the current FE modeling process remains labor-intensive and lacks automation. This paper presents the Human-Enhanced Loop Modeling (HELM) framework, a collaborative human-agent protocol that decomposes long-sequence finite element modeling into discrete, visually verifiable checkpoints across geometry generation, boundary condition definition, and material assignment. The framework is demonstrated through a 20-case matrix of reinforced concrete bridge barriers under MASH TL-4 and TL-5 lateral loading conditions, interfacing specialized agents with two widely used commercial FE softwares, i.e., ANSYS and LS-PrePost. Experimental results show that HELM improves the baseline autonomous modeling success rate from 20% to 75%, with agent-level pass rates for geometry and boundary condition tasks approximately doubling. Error analysis reveals that spatial reasoning and algebraic logic limitations constitute the primary failure modes, underscoring the value of structured human-in-the-loop intervention for modeling automation. The complete agent design code and prompts are open-sourced and can be accessed at: https://github.com/SimAgentDev/Ansys-LSPP-AgentKit.

AI.tldr: HELM uses a human–enhanced loop to decompose and verify FE model creation, achieving automated modeling improvements for safety-critical bridges; 20-case test shows success rate rise from 20% to 75% with doubled geometry/boundary-condition accuracy; open-source toolkit.
AI.motivation: Reduce labor-intensive, error-prone FE modeling of safety-critical infrastructure by introducing structured human-in-the-loop guidance and discrete checkpoints.
AI.method: Decompose long FE workflows into verifiable checkpoints for geometry generation, boundary condition definition, and material assignment. Implement a collaborative agent framework interfacing ANSYS and LS-PrePost, evaluated on a 20-case matrix of reinforced concrete bridge barriers under MASH TL-4/5 loading; measure autonomous agent pass rates and human-in-the-loop improvements.
AI.result: Baseline autonomous modeling success improved from 20% to 75%; agent-level pass rates for geometry and boundary condition tasks roughly doubled; error analysis identifies spatial reasoning and algebraic logic as primary failure modes; complete agent design code and prompts released open-source.
AI.conclusion: Structured human-in-the-loop automation significantly enhances FE modeling of safety-critical infrastructure, highlighting remaining cognitive challenges and providing a reusable framework and open-source resources for broader adoption.

title: DAM-VLA: Decoupled Asynchronous Multimodal Vision Language Action model
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models inherit a shared synchronous clock from vision-language pretraining, processing every input at one rate. This is misaligned with physical interaction, where a high-frequency modality changes at hundreds of hertz, vision evolves more slowly, and language stays constant across an episode. A synchronous VLA oversamples slow modalities, undersamples fast ones, and caps action generation at the lowest effective frequency. We hypothesize that decoupling temporal processing per modality, letting each update and retain information at its own sensor rate, yields stronger representations and more robust control. We present DAM-VLA, which maintains per-modality latent buffers refreshed at sensor rates and read continuously by the action head, integrating new high-frequency modalities through gated cross-attention that leaves the pretrained backbone intact. Across seven contact-rich real-world manipulation tasks, DAM-VLA more than doubles the average success rate of the strongest synchronous baseline (95.2\% vs.\ 40.95\%) while sustaining smooth, reactive 100\,Hz control. Project website: \href{https://intuitive-robots.github.io/DAM-VLA/}{intuitive-robots.github.io/DAM-VLA/}

AI.tldr: Decouples per-modality temporal processing in Vision-Language-Action, using per-modality buffers refreshed at sensor rates and gated cross-attention to fuse high-frequency inputs, achieving superior real-world manipulation performance (significant improvement over synchronous baselines).
AI.motivation: Synchronous VLA processes all inputs at a single rate, causing temporal misalignment with the inherent dynamics of modalities (fast proprioception/tactile, slower vision, constant language), which can degrade representations and control.
AI.method: DAM-VLA maintains per-modality latent buffers updated at respective sensor rates, read continuously by the action head, and integrates new high-frequency modalities via gated cross-attention while keeping the pretrained backbone fixed.
