AI.conclusion: The gamified inversion provides a framework to study authenticity versus efficiency in AI-assisted creativity and to understand when users retain creative voice.

title: Parameter-Efficient Adapter Tuning for Tabular-Image Multimodal Learning
summary: Tabular-image multimodal learning aims to improve predictive modeling by jointly using structured tabular attributes and visual data. Although pretrained encoders provide strong modality-specific representations, full fine-tuning can be computationally expensive, while keeping encoders frozen may limit task-specific adaptation. We propose the Tabular-Image Adapter (TI-Adapter), a modality-specific adapter-based fine-tuning framework for efficient multimodal adaptation. TI-Adapter freezes the pretrained tabular encoder and learns an adapter after the extracted tabular embedding, while adapting the image branch with embedding-level and bottleneck-level adapters instead of full fine-tuning. Experiments on 20 tabular-image datasets show that TI-Adapter achieves competitive or better predictive performance than full fine-tuning while using substantially fewer trainable parameters. Ablation studies further demonstrate the importance of adapter placement for balancing performance and practical efficiency.

AI.tldr: TI-Adapter enables efficient multimodal fine-tuning by inserting adapters in both tabular and image branches, achieving competitive performance with far fewer trainable parameters while freezing the tabular encoder.
AI.motivation: Multimodal learning with pretrained encoders benefits from task adaptation, but full fine-tuning is computationally expensive and often unnecessary. A lightweight adapter-based approach can balance performance and efficiency.
AI.method: Freeze the pretrained tabular encoder and insert an adapter after the tabular embedding. For the image branch, employ embedding-level and bottleneck-level adapters rather than full fine-tuning, enabling task-specific adaptation with few trainable parameters.
AI.result: Across 20 tabular-image datasets, TI-Adapter matches or surpasses full fine-tuning while using substantially fewer trainable parameters. Ablation studies underscore the importance of adapter placement for balancing performance and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Adapter-based fine-tuning is effective for tabular-image multimodal tasks, enabling competitive performance with reduced trainable parameters. Proper placement of adapters is crucial for optimizing the trade-off between accuracy and efficiency.

title: APEX: A Network-Native Time-Series Foundation Model for Forecasting and Anomaly Detection for Wireless Edge Operations
comment: 5 pages, 1 figure, 4 tables. Discusses a network-native time-series foundation model for wireless edge operations
summary: Generic time-series foundation models transfer poorly to wireless network telemetry whose signals are bursty, zero-inflated, and coupled across protocol layers. We present APEX, a network-native, decoder-only transformer for forecasting enterprise AP telemetry, and evaluate it on DHCP degradation as a representative network task. APEX is pre-trained on 10-channel multivariate telemetry from ~4,500 production wireless networks (~100K AP time series, 34 metrics per AP), and is available as APEX-Large (269M, cloud) and APEX-Edge (10.5M, edge). On a 192-step (4-day) DHCP degradation benchmark, APEX-Large reduces MAE by 18% over the strongest foundation-model baseline (Toto) and 38% over SARIMA, with anomaly-detection F1 = 0.93, while APEX-Edge enables sub-second, privacy-preserving inference on AP-class edge hardware. These results suggest network-native pre-training is a practical foundation for proactive wireless operations.

AI.tldr: APEX is a network-native, decoder-only transformer for forecasting enterprise AP telemetry; it outperforms a strong foundation-model baseline and SARIMA on DHCP degradation tasks, and enables privacy-preserving edge inference; demonstrates practical network-native pretraining for proactive wireless operations.
AI.motivation: Generic time-series foundation models transfer poorly to wireless network telemetry due to burstiness, zero-inflation, and cross-layer coupling; a network-native pretraining paradigm aims to improve forecasting and anomaly detection for proactive wireless operations.
AI.method: A decoder-only transformer (APEX) pre-trained on 10-channel multivariate telemetry from ~4,500 production wireless networks (~100K AP time series, 34 metrics per AP), with two deployments: APEX-Large (269M, cloud) and APEX-Edge (10.5M, edge). Evaluation on a 192-step (4-day) DHCP degradation benchmark, comparing against Toto (foundation-model baseline) and SARIMA; includes anomaly-detection evaluation (F1).
AI.result: APEX-Large reduces MAE by 18% over Toto and 38% over SARIMA on the 192-step DHCP degradation task; anomaly-detection F1 = 0.93. APEX-Edge enables sub-second, privacy-preserving inference on AP-class edge hardware.
AI.conclusion: Network-native pre-training is a practical foundation for proactive wireless operations, enabling improved forecasting and rapid, privacy-preserving edge inference.

title: CHORUS: Decentralized Multi-Embodiment Collaboration with One VLA Policy
summary: Multi-robot collaboration allows robots to efficiently take on a wide range of tasks, from moving a couch through a doorway to assembling structures on a construction site. However, achieving such coordination in mobile multi-robot settings remains challenging: centralized methods conditioned on the combined observations of a team scale poorly with team size, and decentralized methods that train one policy per robot often require explicit alignment procedures or information sharing at inference time to overcome partial observability. Our key insight is that the visuomotor priors of pretrained vision-language-action (VLA) models should enable reactive, decentralized collaboration from each robot's local observations alone, without these inference-time assumptions. We propose CHORUS, a framework that adapts a single VLA backbone to control diverse, multi-robot teams. At inference time, each robot runs an independent copy of CHORUS, conditioned only on its own observations and a robot-identifying prompt. In real-world experiments including mobile tape measurement, library book handovers, and laundry basket lifting, CHORUS achieves a 64% point improvement over decentralized, from-scratch models, improves reactivity to teammate behavior by 40% points, and outperforms centralized baselines. Together, these results show that a shared VLA backbone is capable of achieving decentralized multi-robot collaboration, without per-robot policies or inter-robot communication at inference.

AI.tldr: CHORUS adapts a single vision-language-action (VLA) backbone to decentralized multi-robot control, enabling each robot to operate from local observations with a robot-id prompt; achieves strong real-world performance without inference-time inter-robot communication.
AI.motivation: Centralized coordination scales poorly with team size; decentralized methods with per-robot policies require explicit alignment or sharing at inference. Leveraging pretrained visuomotor priors from VLA models can enable reactive, decentralized collaboration from local observations.
AI.method: Adapt a single VLA backbone to control multiple robots. At inference, each robot runs an independent copy of CHORUS, conditioned only on its own observations and a robot-identifying prompt. No inter-robot communication or per-robot policies are needed.
AI.result: In real-world experiments (mobile tape measurement, library book handovers, laundry basket lifting), CHORUS achieves a 64 percentage-point improvement over decentralized, from-scratch models, improves reactivity to teammate behavior by 40 percentage points, and outperforms centralized baselines.
AI.conclusion: A shared VLA backbone can enable decentralized multi-robot collaboration without per-robot policies or inference-time communication, suggesting strong generality and practicality across tasks.

title: Reason, Then Re-reason: Cross-view Revisiting Improves Spatial Reasoning
summary: Spatial reasoning from egocentric videos is inherently challenging because the observable evidence is constrained by the camera trajectory. Existing methods rely on single-turn inference, forcing models to resolve geometric ambiguity through semantic priors rather than verifiable evidence. We argue that spatial reasoning should be revisitable: conclusions formed under limited evidence should remain open to revision when complementary viewpoints become available. Building on this insight, we propose Reason, then Re-reason (ReRe), a training-free, inference-time framework with two phases: in the Reason Phase, an MLLM forms a spatial hypothesis from the original video; in the Re-reason Phase, it verifies or revises the hypothesis by observing a synthesized novel-view video. To enable effective cross-view revisiting, we design a Geometry-to-Video pipeline that renders strategically complementary novel views from predicted 3D geometry. These views feature an elevated, oblique perspective with scene-spanning coverage, while preserving the MLLM's native video interface without architectural modifications. Extensive evaluations on VSI-Bench and STI-Bench demonstrate that ReRe substantially boosts open-source MLLMs to rival proprietary state-of-the-art performance. Project page: https://zhenjiemao.github.io/ReRe/

AI.tldr: Training-free, two-phase inference for egocentric spatial reasoning: Reason forms a spatial hypothesis from the original video, then Re-reason revises it using synthesized novel views generated from predicted 3D geometry via a Geometry-to-Video pipeline. No model training required and the method elevates open-source MLLMs toward proprietary SOTA on VSI-Bench and STI-Bench.
AI.motivation: Single-turn inference on egocentric videos is prone to geometric ambiguity; revisiting conclusions with complementary viewpoints can improve reliability and accuracy.
AI.method: Reason Phase: LLM-based spatial hypothesis from the original video. Re-reason Phase: synthesize oblique, scene-spanning novel-view video from predicted 3D geometry and use it to verify/revise the hypothesis. Geometry-to-Video pipeline renders complementary views while preserving the MLLM's native video interface, requiring no architectural changes. Training-free, inference-time framework.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on VSI-Bench and STI-Bench show substantial boosts for open-source MLLMs, rivaling proprietary SOTA performance.
AI.conclusion: Revisiting spatial reasoning with cross-view evidence via a geometry-to-video synthesis pipeline can close the gap between open and closed models; the approach is training-free and inference-time.

title: GraphInfer-Bench: Benchmarking LLM's Inference Capability on Graphs
summary: Graph analysis underlies many applications whose answers cannot be looked up in a single record or retrieved along a path: laundering rings, drug repurposing, user preference, and scientific theme are all inferred from a node together with its neighbourhood. We introduce GraphInfer-Bench, a benchmark for whether LLMs can perform this graph inference: producing an open-ended answer that no single node supports and no path retrieves. Existing graph-QA protocols cannot test this capability: algorithm simulation, node classification, single-node description, KG-QA, and GraphRAG all admit answers retrievable from one node or along a path. GraphInfer-Bench defines five tasks along Description (what a region is) and Comparison (how regions differ), each constructed so the ground truth lives in no single node. The release contains 42,000 samples across six real-world graphs, produced automatically and screened by a four-layer quality-control protocol. We evaluate four method families against the same tasks: graph-token alignment models, zero-shot frontier closed-source LLMs, Graph2Text supervised fine-tuning, and plain GNNs as a structural reference. No method family closes the gap. Graph-token alignment partially handles description tasks (relational, theme) but collapses on comparison tasks. Frontier LLMs lead on outlier detection and community partition among LLM-based methods but lag on masked-node prediction. Graph2Text SFT is the strongest LLM-based method on the description side yet falls behind frontier LLMs on comparison. Across every task, plain GNNs match or beat the strongest LLM-based row, with the largest margin on community detection. GraphInfer-Bench surfaces graph inference as an open capability gap rather than a property of any one architecture.

AI.tldr: GraphInfer-Bench tests whether LLMs can perform graph inference where ground truth isn't confined to a single node or a path. It introduces five tasks (Description and Comparison) across six real-world graphs (42k samples). Four method families are evaluated; none fully closes the gap, with plain GNNs often outperforming LLM-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing graph-QA protocols rely on information retrievable from a single node or along a path, failing to test true graph inference. The work aims to formalize a benchmark that requires aggregating neighborhood-level information to answer open-ended questions about regions in graphs.
AI.method: Construct five tasks spanning Description and Comparison with ground truth distributed across neighborhoods rather than a single node. Generate 42,000 samples across six real-world graphs via automatic generation and enforce a four-layer quality-control protocol. Evaluate four method families: graph-token alignment models, zero-shot frontier closed-source LLMs, Graph2Text supervised fine-tuning, and plain GNNs as a baseline.
AI.result: No method closes the gap. Graph-token alignment aids Description but struggles with Comparison. Frontier LLMs excel at outlier detection and community partition but lag on masked-node prediction. Graph2Text SFT is strongest among LLM-based methods for Description but behind frontier LLMs on Comparison. Plain GNNs match or beat the strongest LLM-based approach across tasks, especially in community detection.
AI.conclusion: GraphInfer-Bench reveals graph inference as an open capability gap not captured by any single architecture, underscoring the need for new methods that can perform neighborhood-centric reasoning beyond node- or path-retrieval.

title: Ambient Diffusion Policy: Imitation Learning from Suboptimal Data in Robotics
comment: 14 pages (main body), 52 pages total. Project website: https://ambient-diffusion-policy.github.io/
summary: We propose Ambient Diffusion Policy, a simple and principled method for imitation learning from suboptimal data in robotics. High-quality, task-specific robot data is expensive and time-consuming to collect, while suboptimal datasets with lower-quality or out-of-distribution demonstrations are abundant. Existing methods that co-train on both data sources in robotics often fail to separate the meaningful and the harmful features in the suboptimal samples. In contrast, our method extracts only the useful features by introducing a new axis to co-training in robotics: noise-dependent data usage. Ambient Diffusion Policy restricts the contribution of suboptimal data during training to only the high and low diffusion times. To rigorously justify our approach, we first observe that robot action data exhibits a spectral power law. This induces two important properties on the optimal Diffusion Policy that we exploit: a global-to-local hierarchy and locality. We theoretically formalize this discussion using a simplified model. Our experiments validate Ambient Diffusion Policy on four types of suboptimal action data (noisy trajectories, sim-to-real gap, task mismatch, and large-scale data mixtures) across six tasks. The results show that it effectively learns from arbitrary sources of suboptimal data. Notably, it outperforms existing co-training baselines by up to 33% when scaled to Open X-Embodiment - a large dataset with heterogeneous data quality and unstructured distribution shifts. Overall, Ambient Diffusion Policy increases the utility of suboptimal demonstrations and expands the set of usable data sources in robotics.

AI.tldr: Ambient Diffusion Policy introduces a diffusion-model based imitation learning method that leverages suboptimal data by restricting their influence to extreme diffusion times, enabling robust learning from heterogeneous, noisy, or out-of-distribution demonstrations.
AI.motivation: Robotics tasks face limited high-quality, task-specific data while abundant suboptimal data. Existing co-training methods often conflate signal and noise, hindering learning from suboptimal sources. A principled approach that can utilize diverse data sources is needed.
AI.method: Proposes Ambient Diffusion Policy, a diffusion-model-based imitation learner that uses noise-dependent data usage: suboptimal data contribute only at high and low diffusion times. Supports with a spectral power-law observation of robot action data and formalizes global-to-local hierarchy and locality via a simplified theoretical model. Empirically evaluated on six tasks with four suboptimal data types (noisy trajectories, sim-to-real gap, task mismatch, large-scale mixtures) and on Open X-Embodiment dataset.
AI.result: Outperforms co-training baselines by up to 33% on heterogeneous data sources and demonstrates robust learning from arbitrary suboptimal data across six tasks and four suboptimal data types, indicating broad applicability to real-world robotics data collections.
AI.conclusion: Ambient Diffusion Policy broadens the usable data space for imitation learning in robotics, offering a simple, principled framework that leverages diffusion dynamics to separate useful signal from harmful information in suboptimal demonstrations; supported by theoretical insights and strong empirical gains.

title: APT: Action Expert Pretraining Improves Instruction Generalization of Vision-Language-Action Policies
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models that couple pretrained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) with continuous action experts have achieved strong manipulation performance, yet generalization to out-of-distribution (OOD) language instructions remains poor. A known challenge is the structural imbalance in VLA data, where language is far less diverse than visual and action content, making policies prone to visual shortcuts. While discrete-action methods mitigate this through vision-language co-training, continuous action experts lack such protection: they start from random initialization and learn entirely from imbalanced data, producing noisy gradients that corrupt the VLM and fail to exploit its language capability. We address this from a Bayesian perspective, factorizing the policy into a language-agnostic Vision-Action (VA) prior and a language-conditioned VLA likelihood, and propose APT, a two-stage training method emphasizing Action expert PreTraining. In Stage 1, the action expert is pretrained as a VA prior on vision-action pairs from a frozen VLM, bypassing the language imbalance. In Stage 2, language tokens are injected through a gated fusion mechanism that integrates VLM features while preserving the learned visuomotor prior. APT applies to mainstream VLA architectures, including the $π$ and GR00T-style architectures. Comprehensive experiments validate that APT achieves consistent gains on unseen instructions and compositional tasks. Project Page: https://xukechun.github.io/papers/APT/

AI.tldr: Two-stage training (APT) improves Vision-Language-Action models by pretraining an action expert as a vision-action prior on a frozen VLM, then integrating language via gated fusion to preserve visuomotor priors, addressing language imbalance and noisy gradients. It is compatible with π and GR00T-style architectures and yields gains on unseen instructions and compositional tasks.
AI.motivation: VLA data suffer from structural imbalance: language is less diverse than vision/action, causing policies to rely on visual shortcuts. Continuous-action experts trained from random initialization receive noisy gradients and do not leverage VLM language capabilities, unlike discrete-action methods that benefit from co-training.
AI.method: Factorize policy into a language-agnostic VA prior and a language-conditioned VLA likelihood. Stage 1 pretrains the action expert as a VA prior using vision-action pairs from a frozen VLM. Stage 2 injects language via a gated fusion mechanism that combines VLM features with the visuomotor prior, preserving the learned VA prior. Applicable to mainstream VLA architectures including π and GR00T-style designs.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments show that APT yields consistent improvements on unseen instructions and compositional tasks across evaluated VLA setups.
AI.conclusion: APT provides a principled Bayesian decomposition for VLA policies, protecting the visuomotor prior from language imbalance while enabling language-conditioning, leading to better generalization and compatibility with existing architectures.

title: UniIntervene: Agentic Intervention for Efficient Real-World Reinforcement Learning
summary: Human-in-the-loop reinforcement learning (HiL-RL) has emerged as an effective paradigm for real-world robotic manipulation, enabling online policy improvement with human guidance. However, current HiL-RL frameworks remain intervention-intensive, relying on frequent human corrections to redirect the policy out of unproductive exploration, which incurs high labor cost and limits real-world scalability. To address this, we propose UniIntervene, an agentic intervention model that detects unproductive exploration and autonomously recovers the policy toward high-value states, taking over the bulk of interventions from human operators. Specifically, UniIntervene first performs future-conditioned action-value estimation, predicting the latent consequence of the current action and evaluating its induced value, which provides a more stable progress signal. Building on this, a temporal value-risk critic aggregates recent value dynamics and triggers intervention when the estimated value exhibits sustained stagnation or degradation. When intervention is required, UniIntervene retrieves a high-value recovery target from a memory of past intervention episodes and produces executable corrective actions through a goal-conditioned recovery policy. In this way, UniIntervene turns intervention from passive human correction into a value-aware recovery process for efficient real-world RL. Extensive experiments on diverse real-world manipulation tasks demonstrate that UniIntervene improves the average success rate by 8.6% while reducing human interventions by 57% relative to state-of-the-art HiL-RL baselines.

AI.tldr: UniIntervene autonomizes interventions in HiL-RL by using future-value estimates and a value-risk critic to trigger autonomous recovery and a memory-based recovery policy, achieving fewer human interventions while improving success.
AI.motivation: Current human-in-the-loop reinforcement learning (HiL-RL) for real-world robotic manipulation is intervention-heavy, incurring high labor costs and limiting scalability. There is a need for autonomous, value-aware intervention mechanisms to improve efficiency and practicality.
AI.method: Key components: (1) future-conditioned action-value estimation to predict latent consequences and induced value; (2) temporal value-risk critic that aggregates recent value dynamics to detect stagnation or degradation and trigger intervention; (3) memory of past intervention episodes to retrieve high-value recovery targets; (4) goal-conditioned recovery policy that generates executable corrective actions for recovery.
AI.result: Experiments on diverse real-world manipulation tasks show UniIntervene increases average success rate by 8.6% and reduces human interventions by 57% relative to state-of-the-art HiL-RL baselines.
AI.conclusion: UniIntervene demonstrates that turning human interventions into autonomous, value-aware recovery processes can substantially improve the efficiency and scalability of real-world RL without sacrificing performance.

title: Ouroboros-Spatial: Closing the Data-Model Loop for Spatial Reasoning
summary: Spatial reasoning remains a persistent challenge for multimodal large language models (MLLMs). Existing approaches largely rely on large-scale, statically curated datasets, where all training samples are treated uniformly regardless of the model's evolving capabilities. This static paradigm is inherently data-inefficient: training capacity is often spent on samples that are either trivial or overly difficult for the model at its current stage. To address this limitation, we propose Ouroboros-Spatial, a self-evolving training framework in which the model plays dual roles as a proposer and a solver. In each iteration, a frozen proposer generates spatial question-answer (QA) pairs from 3D scene metadata and raw video frames, together with executable code for deriving reliable ground truth. A learnable solver is then fine-tuned on the accepted samples, and its per-sample prediction confidence is used as a difficulty signal. This signal is fed back to the proposer in the next iteration, guiding it to generate questions better matched to the solver's current capabilities. Through this closed-loop design, the training distribution co-evolves with model ability, reducing redundant trivial examples while filtering out ambiguous or uninformative samples with limited learning value. Across six spatial reasoning benchmarks, Ouroboros-Spatial substantially improves Qwen3-VL-4B and Qwen3-VL-8B while using an order of magnitude fewer training examples than recent large-scale curated datasets. On VSI-Bench, it yields absolute gains of 9.9 and 6.8 points for the 4B and 8B models, respectively, enabling both to outperform a wide range of strong open-source and proprietary baselines.

AI.tldr: Ouroboros-Spatial introduces a self-evolving training loop for multimodal LLMs, where a frozen proposer creates spatial QA pairs with ground-truth derivations, a learnable solver is fine-tuned on accepted samples, and the solver's per-sample confidence guides the next cycle. This closed-loop co-evolution reduces data waste and improves spatial reasoning performance with far fewer training examples.
AI.motivation: Static, curated datasets for spatial reasoning in multimodal LLMs are data-inefficient and misaligned with the model's evolving capabilities. A self-adaptive training paradigm can concentrate learning on samples that are most informative for the current model, enabling more efficient data use and continual improvement.
AI.method: In each iteration, the proposer, kept frozen, generates spatial QA pairs from 3D scene metadata and raw video frames and provides executable code to derive ground truth. A learnable solver is fine-tuned on the accepted samples; its per-sample prediction confidence serves as a difficulty signal that is fed back to the proposer to tailor future questions to the solver's current capabilities. The loop co-evolves the training distribution with model ability; experiments span six spatial reasoning benchmarks and two Qwen-based VL models (4B and 8B).
AI.result: Ouroboros-Spatial yields substantial gains across six benchmarks, notably achieving absolute gains of 9.9 and 6.8 points on VSI-Bench for the 4B and 8B models, respectively, while requiring an order of magnitude fewer training examples than recent large-scale curated datasets, and enabling both models to surpass multiple strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: A closed-loop, self-evolving training framework efficiently aligns data generation with model capabilities, mitigating data waste, improving spatial reasoning in multimodal LLMs, and delivering strong performance with significantly reduced data requirements.

title: DeMix: Debugging Training Data with Mixed Data Error Types by Investigating Influence Vectors
summary: High-quality training data is essential for the success of machine learning models. However, real-world datasets often contain mixed types of errors arising from systematic flaws in data preparation pipelines, including label errors, feature errors, and spurious correlations. Effective debugging of training data requires both detecting erroneous samples and identifying their specific error types to enable targeted repair, yet existing data cleaning and attribution methods fail to adequately address this dual requirement. In this paper, we propose DeMix, a novel framework that simultaneously diagnoses erroneous samples and their error types. Our key insight is that different error types produce distinct patterns on model behavior. DeMix captures such error-specific patterns by influence vectors that characterize how each training sample affects model predictions across all validation samples. We formulate training data debugging as a multi-label classification problem where a classifier is developed to predict error types directly from influence vectors. We further introduce an intervention-based learning strategy that guides the classifier to capture invariant rationales specific to each error type, ensuring the learned classifier generalizes effectively. Empirical evaluations on 11 tasks across tabular data prediction, recommendation systems, and LLM alignment demonstrate that DeMix significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, achieving a 22.61% improvement in data debugging F1-score and a 9.32% gain in task model performance after data repair. Code is available at: https://github.com/SJTU-DMTai/DeMix.

AI.tldr: DeMix is a data-debugging framework that uses influence vectors to relate training samples to validation predictions, enabling multi-label error-type classification and an intervention-based training strategy to discover invariant error-specific rationales for robust data repair across diverse tasks.
AI.motivation: Real-world datasets contain mixed error types (label, feature, spurious correlations) that degrade model performance. Existing methods struggle to both detect erroneous samples and identify error types, hindering targeted data repair. DeMix seeks to jointly diagnose samples and their error types.
AI.method: Compute influence vectors that quantify each training sample's effect on predictions over validation samples. Treat error-type diagnosis as a multi-label classification problem using these influence vectors as features. Introduce intervention-based learning to learn invariant rationales for each error type, improving generalization across datasets.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 11 tasks spanning tabular data, recommender systems, and LLM alignment shows DeMix outperforms state-of-the-art data debugging methods, with a 22.61% improvement in data debugging F1-score and 9.32% improvement in task model performance after data repair.
AI.conclusion: DeMix provides a unified, generalizable approach to both detecting erroneous samples and attributing them to specific error types, enabling targeted data repair and improved downstream performance.

title: Multimodal Ordinal Modeling of Alzheimer's Disease Severity Using Structural MRI and Clinical Data
comment: 18 pages. Submitted to journal for review
summary: Neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease (AD) require accurate and scalable tools for assessing disease severity, yet current clinical staging remains time-intensive and prone to variability. We propose an attention-enhanced multimodal machine learning framework with ordinal regression for automated and interpretable AD severity staging. The framework integrates T1-weighted MRI with demographic and genetic variables and compares unimodal and multimodal architectures using ordinal and non-ordinal prediction heads. Models were trained and validated using cohort-stratified splits derived from the ADNI, AIBL, and NIFD datasets. A strictly held-out test set was constructed using subjects excluded from all training, validation, preprocessing, and hyperparameter tuning procedures, with subject-level splitting employed throughout to prevent data leakage. Among unimodal approaches, the T1-weighted MRI model achieved slightly higher adjacent-stage accuracy (0.963) and agreement with clinical staging (QWK 0.444) than the tabular model (QWK 0.433). Integrating imaging, demographic, and genetic information improved overall performance. The multimodal non-ordinal baseline achieved the lowest prediction error (MAE 0.340), whereas the ordinal multimodal model achieved the highest adjacent-stage accuracy (0.970) and strongest agreement with clinical staging (QWK 0.549). These findings indicate that ordinal formulations better capture the ordered structure of the CDR scale and yield predictions more consistent with clinical staging. Explainability analyses using Grad CAM++ and SHAP demonstrated anatomically and clinically plausible model behavior, supporting transparent decision-making. Overall, attention-based multimodal learning with ordinal regression represents a robust, interpretable, and scalable approach for automated AD severity staging and AI-assisted clinical decision support.

AI.tldr: Attention-enhanced multimodal framework with ordinal regression improves AD severity staging using T1 MRI, demographics, and genetics; ordinal approach outperforms non-ordinal and unimodal models; explainability via Grad-CAM++ and SHAP supports clinical interpretability.
AI.motivation: Address the need for accurate, scalable, and interpretable automated staging of Alzheimer's disease severity, leveraging multimodal data and ordinal relationships in the clinical scale (CDR).
AI.method: Multimodal architecture combining T1-weighted MRI with demographic and genetic variables; compared unimodal and multimodal setups with ordinal and non-ordinal prediction heads; cohort-stratified training/validation on ADNI, AIBL, NIFD; strictly held-out test set with subject-level splitting; Grad CAM++ and SHAP for explainability.
AI.result: Unimodal MRI achieved higher adjacent-stage accuracy (0.963) and QWK (0.444) than the tabular model (QWK 0.433). Multimodal integration improved performance; non-ordinal baseline had lowest MAE (0.340). The ordinal multimodal model achieved the highest adjacent-stage accuracy (0.970) and QWK (0.549), indicating better capture of the CDR’s ordered structure. Explainability analyses showed anatomically/clinically plausible patterns.
AI.conclusion: Ordinal formulations in attention-based multimodal learning yield more accurate and clinically aligned AD severity predictions, with transparent decision-making enabled by Grad CAM++ and SHAP, supporting AI-assisted clinical decision support.

title: From Uniform to Learned Graph Priors: Diffusion for Structure Discovery
summary: Neural relational inference (NRI) methods discover interaction graphs from trajectories through variational reasoning on discrete potential edges. However, these methods typically rely on oversimplified, factorized graph priors. Such priors, typically nearing uniform distributions, treat edges as independent entities. This systemic misalignment does not match the real-world systems and yields diffuse and indecisive edge posteriors limiting the reliability of structural discovery. To address this, we propose \textit{Diff-prior}, a diffusion-parameterized adaptive prior used to calibrate latent graph distribution rather than generate graphs. Our core insight is to reframe prior integration as a learnable denoising-style calibration that organizes scattered, uncertain edge posteriors into a more reliable overall structure which can be trained by the diffusion model. Diff-prior learns an adaptive structure prior that performs structured calibration on the edge posteriors during inference, guiding it towards a distribution closer to the underlying structure. The diff-prior operates before structural sampling and acts as a denoising calibrator directly on the encoder edge distribution, which provides a generic training paradigm over structured variables. Experiments on standard benchmarks validated our framework, and the results indicate that Diff-prior improves the performance of structure inference and generates more decisive edge posteriors across multiple NRI-family architectures. The code is available on https://github.com/Hardy158118/Diffprior.

AI.tldr: Diff-prior: a diffusion-based adaptive prior to calibrate edge posteriors in neural relational inference, yielding more decisive structure discovery.
AI.motivation: Current NRI priors are oversimplified and factorized, treating edges independently and often near-uniform. This mismatch yields diffuse, indecisive edge posteriors and unreliable structural discovery.
AI.method: Introduce Diff-prior, a diffusion-parameterized adaptive prior that calibrates latent graph distributions by denoising-style conditioning on encoder edge posteriors before sampling. It learns a structured calibration and serves as a generic training paradigm for structured variables.
AI.result: Empirical benchmarks show improved structure inference and more decisive edge posteriors across multiple NRI-family architectures; code provided.
AI.conclusion: Diff-prior provides a trainable calibration mechanism for structured latent variables, improving reliability of graph discovery in NRI and potentially generalizable to other structured latent-variable settings.

title: Flow Matching with In-Context Priors for Out-of-Distribution Brain Dynamics
comment: Code and pretrained models available at https://github.com/SamGijsen/pinc-flows
summary: Flow matching and diffusion models enable conditional generation across domains ranging from images to proteins, with recent extensions to out-of-distribution contexts. Yet generative models of neural time series have largely remained restricted to categorical conditioning, precluding compositional and zero-shot generalization. In this work, we propose a per-timestep conditioned diffusion transformer for generating realistic fMRI brain dynamics during unseen cognitive tasks by injecting both compositional language and optional spatial priors in-context. Such zero-shot generation could enable counterfactual neuroscience by supporting in-silico design and evaluation of novel cognitive experiments before empirical validation. Leveraging this model, we evaluate across hundreds of held-out task conditions and characterize predictive performance in relation to the training manifold. From language alone, the model recovers region-specific recruitment across tasks and held-out spatial activation patterns. Spatial priors, when available, complement the text pathway by anchoring generation in regions of task space where language alone degrades, while retaining the compositional structure needed for counterfactual task specification. To our knowledge this is the first generative model of whole-cortex fMRI dynamics for unseen cognitive tasks, advancing counterfactual neuroscience and data-driven experimental design.

AI.tldr: A per-timestep conditioned diffusion transformer that generates whole-cortex fMRI dynamics for unseen cognitive tasks using language conditioning and optional spatial priors, enabling zero-shot and counterfactual neuroscience.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of neural time-series generative models that are restricted to categorical conditioning, hindering compositional and zero-shot generalization across cognitive tasks and experiments; enable in-silico counterfactual neuroscience and experimental design.
AI.method: Introduce a per-timestep conditioned diffusion transformer conditioned on language prompts describing cognitive tasks and optional spatial priors provided in-context; model generates realistic fMRI dynamics; trained/evaluated on fMRI data across many tasks; assesses predictive performance relative to training manifold and qualitative activation patterns.
AI.result: Language conditioning recovers region-specific recruitments across tasks; spatial priors anchor generation in task-space regions where language alone fails; the model preserves compositional structure enabling counterfactual task specification; evaluated on hundreds of held-out tasks showing alignment with held-out activation patterns.
AI.conclusion: Presents the first generative model of whole-cortex fMRI dynamics for unseen cognitive tasks, enabling counterfactual neuroscience and data-driven experimental design.

title: From Nominal Intensity to Equivalent Rainfall: A Path-Based Credibility Evaluation Framework for Simulated Rainfall in Autonomous-Driving Perception Tests
summary: Credible simulated-rainfall conditions are essential for identifying perception-system boundaries and supporting SOTIF-oriented risk assessment in automated driving. However, closed-field tests are often described only by nominal rainfall intensity or single-point measurements, making it difficult to align simulated rain fields with real rainfall and map test results to real-world scenarios. This paper proposes a path-based credibility evaluation method for simulated rainfall in autonomous-driving perception tests. Using the drop size and velocity joint distribution of real rainfall as the reference, each candidate path is represented by path-equivalent rainfall intensity, an uncertainty band, and a path-averaged Realism of Raindrop Distribution (RRD) score. Lidar target point-cloud count and mean reflectivity are further used for perception-consistency correction, quantifying the proxy capability of each simulated-rainfall path for real-rainfall perception effects. Experiments are conducted using about 10,000 real-rainfall raindrop-spectrum samples, 728 RainSense perception samples, and 45 spatial sampling points in a 2.4 m x 7.2 m simulated-rainfall area. Results show that spatial non-uniformity remains under the same nominal condition, confirming the need for path-based evaluation. The method identifies Path IV and Path VI as preferable candidates, with results of 11.54 +/- 0.31 mm/h, RRD = 0.43, and 8.28 +/- 0.34 mm/h, RRD = 0.46, respectively. These paths show more balanced performance in rainfall-intensity stability, raindrop-spectrum realism, and perception consistency. The proposed method supports path selection, condition description, and credible interpretation of autonomous-driving perception tests under rainfall.

AI.tldr: A path-based credibility evaluation framework for simulated rainfall in autonomous-driving perception tests, aligning simulations with real rainfall using drop-size/velocity distributions, with path metrics (path-equivalent rainfall intensity, uncertainty band, RRD), and perception-consistency calibration via lidar metrics; experiments identify Path IV and VI as preferable candidates balancing rainfall stability, distribution realism, and perception effects.
AI.motivation: Current closed-field rainfall tests rely on nominal intensity or single-point measures, which hampers alignment with real rainfall and the credibility of perception tests under rainfall. A path-based evaluation framework is proposed to capture spatial non-uniformity and realism of raindrop distributions for SOTIF-oriented risk assessment.
AI.method: Represent each simulated-rainfall path by path-equivalent rainfall intensity, an uncertainty band, and a path-averaged Realism of Raindrop Distribution (RRD) score, using real rainfall drop-size and velocity distributions as reference. Apply perception-consistency correction using lidar target-point count and mean reflectivity. Evaluate about 10,000 raindrop-spectrum samples, 728 perception samples, and 45 spatial points in a 2.4 m x 7.2 m area to identify preferable paths.
AI.result: Path IV and Path VI emerge as preferable: Path IV ~11.54±0.31 mm/h with RRD 0.43; Path VI ~8.28±0.34 mm/h with RRD 0.46, indicating balanced rainfall-intensity stability, raindrop-spectrum realism, and perception-consistency.
AI.conclusion: The path-based credibility method enables path selection and credible interpretation of autonomous-driving perception tests under rainfall, improving alignment with real rainfall and supporting SOTIF risk assessment.

title: Fine-tuning Multi-modal LLMs with ART: Art-based Reinforcement Training
summary: There are two main Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) techniques for Large Language Models (LLMs). While Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) introduces additional weights between the LLM layers, Soft Prompting introduces additional fine-tuning-specific raw tokens to an LLM input. However, both require modification to the computational graphs of precompiled, preoptimized LLMs. As a result, neither is fully supported in high-throughput engines like vLLM. We propose fine-tuning with ART (Art-based Reinforcement Training). The method injects information into a frozen Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) by optimizing only its raw visual input, thus enabling the soft-token approach on pre-compiled computational graphs. It relies on backpropagation of gradients back into a plain pixel array and thus supports any fine-tuning objective. Moreover, the optimized visual input can be stylized as task-relevant computational artworks. The approach's effectiveness is confirmed for different sizes of a popular open Qwen architecture and for several textual benchmarks. Specifically, ART reaches accuracy competitive with LoRA across mathematics and structured-tool-use benchmarks.

AI.tldr: ART enables fine-tuning of frozen multimodal LLMs by optimizing raw visual input, enabling soft-token-like adaptation on precompiled graphs and achieving competitive accuracy to LoRA on math and structured-tool-use benchmarks in Qwen architectures.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods (LoRA, Soft Prompting) that require modifying the computational graph, which is impractical for high-throughput engines with precompiled graphs; provide a graph-free fine-tuning pathway for LLMs.
AI.method: Backpropagate gradients into the raw pixel array of the visual input for a frozen multimodal LLM, injecting task-relevant information without altering the model's parameters or graphs. The visual input can be stylized as computational artworks. The approach supports any fine-tuning objective and is demonstrated on sizes of an open Qwen LLM.
AI.result: ART achieves accuracy competitive with LoRA on mathematics and structured-tool-use benchmarks; validated across different sizes of a popular open Qwen architecture and on textual benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ART broadens the scope of PEFT by enabling soft-token-like tuning on pre-compiled graphs, allowing arbitrary fine-tuning objectives and stylized inputs while preserving the throughput advantages of high-throughput engines.

title: FitVTON: Fit-aware Virtual Try-On via Body-Garment Size Control
summary: While diffusion-based virtual try-on has achieved impressive visual realism, most methods treat the task as 2D inpainting, prioritizing texture preservation over physical plausibility. Consequently, they often produce plausible-looking images that fail to reflect authentic garment fit across diverse body shapes. We present FitVTON, a Fit-aware virtual try-on model on different bodies in the wild. FitVTON encodes garment-body size through structured text prompts, and learn from simulated try-on triplets from parameterized garment model. To improve the fitting effects over garment silhouettes, we introduce two auxiliary head to predict the masks for both the garment and the exposed body. We further introduce a texture rectification stage to improve realistic appearance from simulated data. To evaluate the fitting fidelity, we curate a real-world dataset, FittingEffect3K, combining VLM-based scoring protocol. Both subjective and quantitive experiments show that FitVTON demonstrate authentic fitting fidelity, with significant sizing accuracy and shape preservation over state-of-the-art methods while maintaining competitive image quality. Project Page: https://zenoning.github.io/FitVTON/.

AI.tldr: FitVTON is a diffusion-based virtual try-on model that introduces fit-aware control via structured text prompts and auxiliary masks, plus a texture rectification stage, achieving better garment fit across diverse bodies. evaluated on a new real-world dataset (FittingEffect3K) with VLM-based scoring, outperforming state-of-the-art in fit accuracy while keeping image quality.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-based virtual try-on methods treat the task as 2D inpainting, prioritizing texture preservation but neglecting genuine garment fit across varying body shapes. This leads to plausible textures but inaccurate sizing and silhouette against different physiques. There is a need for explicit fit-aware modeling and guidance to capture garment-body interaction and occlusions.
AI.method: 1) Encode garment-body size through structured text prompts. 2) Learn from simulated try-on triplets using a parameterized garment model. 3) Add two auxiliary heads predicting masks for the garment and the exposed body to improve silhouette fidelity. 4) Introduce a texture rectification stage to enhance realism from simulated data. 5) Curate FittingEffect3K, a real-world dataset, and evaluate with a VLM-based scoring protocol, supported by subjective and quantitative analyses.
AI.result: Experiments show FitVTON achieves authentic fitting fidelity, with significant improvements in sizing accuracy and shape preservation over state-of-the-art, while maintaining competitive image quality.
AI.conclusion: FitVTON advances fit-aware virtual try-on by integrating size-aware prompts, mask-guided refinement, and texture rectification, enabling better garment silhouette fidelity across diverse bodies. A real-world evaluation dataset (FittingEffect3K) supports the reported gains; a project page is provided for access.

title: RePAIR: Predictive Self-Supervised Representation Learning in Chess
comment: Accepted for oral presentation at IEEE Conference on Games 2026
summary: In this paper, we introduce Representation Prediction via Autoencoding using Iterative Refinement (RePAIR) - a novel self-supervised representation learning architecture that synthesizes Masked Autoencoders (MAE), Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA), and Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT). We demonstrate how it can be used to encode objects in sequential data like consecutive chess positions into compact yet meaningful representations. The basic principle of the architecture is to mask large portions of a sequence of latent states, similar to BERT and MAE. Then, we apply a lightweight Predictor to the latent representations that repairs gaps in the sequence in a lower-dimensional embedding space akin to JEPA. Our experiments in the domain of chess show that the Encoder refines the board representations such that meaningful chess concepts emerge clustered in the latent space. Furthermore, reconstructions of the masked board states show that the model is able to reason about the piece movements without relying on costly reinforcement learning methods. Lastly, we find that the resulting representation space allows for quick and intuitive dissections of chess games by observing the game path trajectories in this semantically rich space.

AI.tldr: Repurpose autoencoding with iterative refinement (RePAIR) that masks large portions of a sequence of latent states and uses a lightweight predictor to repair gaps in a lower-dimensional embedding space. Combines MAE, JEPA, and BERT concepts to yield compact, semantically meaningful representations of sequential data, demonstrated on chess where latent representations cluster chess concepts and masked reconstructions reveal reasoning about piece movements without RL.
AI.motivation: Develop a self-supervised, interpretable representation learning framework for sequential data that yields compact, semantically meaningful embeddings, enabling reasoning and analysis of sequences (e.g., chess games) without relying on reinforcement learning.
AI.method: Mask large portions of a latent-state sequence (à la BERT/MAE) and apply a lightweight Predictor to repair gaps in a lower-dimensional embedding space (à la JEPA). The Encoder refines latent-board representations so that meaningful chess concepts emerge and masked states can be reconstructed, combining MAE, JEPA, and BERT-style masking.
AI.result: In chess, the Encoder organizes the latent space so that important chess concepts cluster. Reconstructed masked boards demonstrate the model's ability to reason about piece movements without RL, and the representation space enables intuitive analysis of games via trajectory visualizations.
AI.conclusion: RePAIR offers a unified self-supervised framework that integrates MAE, JEPA, and BERT to produce semantically rich, compact representations for sequential data, enabling reasoning and interpretable analysis (e.g., chess) without reinforcement learning.

title: SpikeTAD: Spiking Neural Networks for End-to-End Temporal Action Detection
summary: Video understanding is a crucial part of computer vision, with numerous application scenarios. With the increasing popularity of mobile devices, an increasing number of efforts are trying to deploy video understanding models on them. However, existing video understanding models are difficult to deploy due to their large size and prohibitive power consumption. Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) have shown bioplausibility and low power advantages over Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), especially on neuromorphic chips which are regarded as essential components of future mobile devices. However, excessively long conversion time-steps and severe performance degradation problems limit their application. To solve the problems above, we explore the application of SNNs on temporal action detection (TAD), which is an important task in video understanding, and propose the first SNN-based end-to-end TAD architecture coined as SpikeTAD. While maintaining extremely low power consumption, SpikeTAD achieves an average mAP of 67.2% in THUMOS14 and 37.42% in ActivityNet-1.3, demonstrating the feasibility of a low-power TAD model. Our code is available at https://github.com/MCG-NJU/SpikeTAD.

AI.tldr: Proposes SpikeTAD, the first end-to-end SNN-based temporal action detector, delivering low-power inference with competitive accuracy on standard benchmarks; code released.
AI.motivation: Mobile video understanding demands lightweight, energy-efficient models. SNNs offer low power and neuromorphic compatibility, but previous SNN approaches suffer from long conversion-time steps and accuracy losses. Applying SNNs to temporal action detection (TAD) aims to enable on-device video analysis with reduced energy use.
AI.method: introduces SpikeTAD, an end-to-end Spiking Neural Network architecture for temporal action detection. The design addresses long conversion-time steps and performance degradation, achieving an end-to-end SNN pipeline for TAD and evaluating on standard datasets.
AI.result: SpikeTAD attains an average mAP of 67.2% on THUMOS14 and 37.42% on ActivityNet-1.3, while maintaining very low power consumption, demonstrating the feasibility of a low-power TAD model. Code is available at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that end-to-end SNN-based temporal action detection is viable for on-device video understanding, offering substantial energy efficiency improvements with competitive accuracy; it establishes SpikeTAD as a baseline for future neuromorphic approaches to video tasks.

title: Beyond representational alignment with brain-guided language models for robust reasoning
summary: The correspondence between large language models (LLMs) and the neural mechanisms underlying human higher-order cognition remains insufficiently characterized. Given that language and reasoning in the human brain appear dissociable, an open question is whether LLMs align with neural signals from reasoning-related regions and whether such signals can improve them. Here, focusing on deductive reasoning, we show that LLM internal representations are not only partially aligned with task-fMRI activity but can also be directly enhanced by these signals. Using a neural-predictivity metric, we find that LLMs explain a substantial fraction of the explainable variance in reasoning-related regions at the aggregate level, whereas predictivity within specific reasoning types is lower, indicating both alignment and divergence. Building on this, we propose a brain-guided framework: we steer model representations along directions induced by the joint structure of model and brain representations, applying intervention at inference and fine-tuning during training. We demonstrate that task-evoked brain signals can directly enhance LLM reasoning, yielding gains orthogonal to language-only supervision across 10 LLMs (1.5B-72B), with transfer across reasoning types and up to 13\% absolute accuracy gain. Our results advance LLM-brain correspondences from correlation to guidance, establishing a brain-signal-driven pathway toward more robust and cognitively aligned AI.

AI.tldr: LLMs align with and can be improved by neural signals of human reasoning; a brain-guided framework yields up to 13% absolute accuracy gains across 10 LLMs via brain-derived interventions.
AI.motivation: To understand how LLMs relate to neural mechanisms of higher-order cognition and whether brain signals can be used to improve LLM reasoning.
AI.method: Evaluate neural predictivity between LLM representations and task-fMRI in reasoning-related regions; develop a brain-guided framework that steers representations along directions induced by joint brain-model structure; apply interventions at inference and during training; test across 10 LLMs (1.5B–72B); assess reasoning performance.
AI.result: LLMs explain a substantial portion of explainable variance in reasoning regions; predictivity varies by specific reasoning types indicating alignment and divergence; brain-guided interventions can enhance reasoning orthogonally to language supervision, with up to 13% absolute accuracy gain and transfer across reasoning types across 10 LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a brain-signal-driven path to more robust, cognitively aligned AI; moves LLM–brain relationships from correlation toward guidance.

title: Vision Transformers for Face Recognition Need More Registers
comment: Accepted at the 20th IEEE International Conference on Automatic Face and Gesture Recognition (2026)
summary: Recent advances in Vision Transformers (ViTs) for face recognition (FR) have moved beyond the standard CLS-token paradigm. In this paradigm, a special classification token (CLS) is prepended to the patch embeddings and used as a representation of the input for downstream tasks. An alternative approach, Concatenated Patch Embeddings (CPE), instead leverages all patch tokens by concatenating them into a single vector, which is then projected into a compact face representation. CPE has been shown to improve recognition performance in comparison to CLS-based ones, but our qualitative analysis of attention maps showed the presence of artifacts that limit their interpretability. To address this issue, we incorporate register tokens, learnable tokens concatenated to the initial patch embeddings, and processed jointly through the ViT encoder blocks. This mechanism has been shown to produce more structured and interpretable attention maps compared to baseline ViT. We empirically demonstrate that these artifacts consistently appear across various ViT backbones, including small and large models, and that introducing register tokens effectively mitigates them. Adding four or eight registers significantly enhances interpretability, with eight registers providing the highest verification accuracies and smoothest attention structures. Our resulting model, ViT-8R, corresponds to a CPE-based ViT-B architecture augmented with eight register tokens achieves state-of-the-art performance among ViT-based FR models on large-scale IJB-B and IJB-C benchmarks. Also, ViT-8R produces substantially clearer attention maps compared with the baseline model, which offer deeper insight into the model's attention behavior (https://github.com/TaharChettaoui/ViT-FR-Registers)

AI.tldr: Introduces learnable register tokens to a concatenated patch embedding (CPE) ViT for face recognition, producing ViT-8R with improved interpretability and state-of-the-art results among ViT FR models on IJB-B/IJB-C.
AI.motivation: Address artifacts and limited interpretability in attention maps of CPE-based ViTs for face recognition, while also boosting verification accuracy.
AI.method: Add learnable register tokens concatenated to initial patch embeddings and processed jointly through ViT encoder blocks; evaluate with 4 or 8 registers, with eight registers forming ViT-8R (CPE-based ViT-B); compare against baseline ViT variants and analyze attention maps.
AI.result: Artifacts in attention maps are mitigated; eight registers yield the best verification accuracy and the smoothest attention structures; ViT-8R achieves state-of-the-art performance among ViT-based FR models on large-scale IJB-B and IJB-C; attention maps are substantially clearer.
AI.conclusion: Register tokens effectively enhance interpretability and recognition performance in CPE-based ViTs for face recognition; ViT-8R demonstrates strong results across model sizes and provides deeper insight into attention behavior.

title: Online Shift Detection and Conformal Adaptation for Deployed Safety Classifiers
comment: 16 pages, 4 figures, 7 tables. Code and data at https://github.com/junwenleong/safety-classifier-shift-monitor
summary: We present an online monitoring system for distributional shift in deployed safety classifiers, using calibrated sequential statistics to detect when a classifier has moved out of distribution. Upon detection, a conformal abstention layer adapts decision thresholds to recover a target error rate epsilon=0.1. In a pre-registered factorial evaluation (4 classifiers x 5 shift conditions x 20 seeds x 2 window sizes, 800 cells), the system achieves 86.6% valid detection (693/800, 95% CI [84.1%, 88.8%]) with mean latency of 39.5 steps. Detection holds across three ground-truth regimes: synthetic onset (86.6%), real temporal jailbreaks (85%, 17/20), and GCG adversarial attacks. Weighted conformal prediction recovers up to 39 pp of lost coverage for DeBERTa (ESS=46/300) but collapses for all other classifiers (ESS~300): logistic density ratio estimation achieves perfect source/target separability in high-dimensional embedding spaces, clipping all importance weights to the floor. DeBERTa shows a gradient from effective correction (paraphrase, ESS=46) to near-total collapse (adversarial suffix, ESS=206). PCA to 32 dimensions breaks the collapse, recovering 33 pp for Llama Guard and 21 pp for ShieldGemma. Variance decomposition reveals classifier (eta^2=0.243), shift type (eta^2=0.237), and their interaction (eta^2=0.185) all contribute substantially to detection latency variance (all p<0.001), indicating per-classifier monitoring profiles are necessary.

AI.tldr: Online monitoring of distributional shift in safety classifiers using calibrated sequential statistics and a conformal abstention layer to maintain target error rate; strong detection performance across shifts, with mixed results for weighted conformal prediction depending on model; PCA helps recover coverage; variance indicates need for per-model monitoring profiles.
AI.motivation: Maintain safety of deployed classifiers by detecting distributional shift in real time and adapting decision thresholds to preserve a target error rate (epsilon = 0.1).
AI.method: Calibrated sequential statistics for online shift detection; upon detection, apply a conformal abstention layer to adjust thresholds to meet epsilon=0.1. Evaluation uses a factorial design (4 classifiers x 5 shift conditions x 20 seeds x 2 window sizes, 800 cells). Assess valid detection rate, detection latency, and robustness across synthetic onset, real temporal jailbreaks, and GCG adversarial attacks. Also investigates weighted conformal prediction (source/target weighting) for DeBERTa (ESS values), including dimensionality reduction (PCA to 32) and per-classifier monitoring; variance decomposition of latency.
AI.result: Achieves 86.6% valid detection (693/800; 95% CI [84.1%, 88.8%]) with mean latency 39.5 steps. Detection stable across ground-truth regimes: synthetic onset 86.6%; real temporal jailbreaks 85% (17/20); GCG adversarial attacks. Weighted conformal prediction recovers up to 39 percentage points of lost coverage for DeBERTa (ESS=46/300) but collapses for other classifiers (ESS~300) due to perfect source/target separability of gradient-based density ratio estimation and clipping of importance weights. DeBERTa performance degrades from effective correction (paraphrase, ESS=46) to near-total collapse (adversarial suffix, ESS=206). PCA to 32 dims breaks collapse, recovering 33 percentage points for Llama Guard and 21 for ShieldGemma. Variance decomposition shows classifier (eta^2=0.243), shift type (0.237), and their interaction (0.185) all significantly contribute to latency variance (p<0.001), implying need for per-classifier monitoring profiles.
AI.conclusion: Monitoring must be tailored to each classifier and shift type; both the abstention mechanism and conformal methods show potential but are sensitive to model architecture and feature space; addressing weight clipping and separability issues, along with dimensionality reduction strategies, is key to robust deployment.

title: Metadata-Aware Multi-Prompt Reasoning for Zero-Shot Accident Understanding
comment: Accepted at the AUTOPILOT Workshop, CVPR 2026 (non-archival). Workshop Paper ID 15
summary: In this paper, we address the problem of zero-shot understanding of accidents from surveillance videos by identifying when an impact event occurs, what type of impact it is, and where in the frame it occurs using natural language. We propose a three-stage pipeline that decomposes the accident understanding into when, what, and where. The first stage extracts a short temporal window around the impact using vision-language similarity. In the second stage, we perform metadata-driven multi-prompt reasoning with five complementary views (baseline, motion, geometry, contrast, and tiebreaker) and resolve disagreement via an entropy-gated pairwise adjudicator. Finally, we localize the impact of an open-vocabulary detector queried on the predicted accident type and scene layout, and aggregate detections across keyframes using a score-weighted centroid. Our pipeline achieves a substantial improvement in the harmonic-mean score over a centre-of-frame baseline on the zero-shot ACCIDENT @ CVPR benchmark. We show that decomposing zero-shot video understanding into temporal localization, semantic classification, and spatial grounding enable more reliable reasoning with vision-language models than direct prompting alone.

AI.tldr: A three-stage zero-shot accident understanding pipeline for surveillance videos that localizes when an impact occurs, classifies its type, and grounds its location using vision-language models; it uses a temporal window via similarity, multi-prompt reasoning with five views and an entropy-gated adjudicator, and an open-vocabulary detector for spatial grounding, achieving strong results on ACCIDENT @ CVPR.
AI.motivation: To enable zero-shot accident understanding by decomposing the task into temporal localization, semantic classification, and spatial grounding, improving reliability of vision-language model reasoning over direct prompting.
AI.method: Stage 1: extract a short temporal window around the impact via vision-language similarity. Stage 2: metadata-driven multi-prompt reasoning with five complementary views (baseline, motion, geometry, contrast, tiebreaker) and entropy-gated pairwise adjudicator to resolve disagreements. Stage 3: localize impact with an open-vocabulary detector queried on predicted accident type and scene layout; aggregate detections across keyframes with a score-weighted centroid.
AI.result: Substantial improvement in harmonic-mean score over a center-of-frame baseline on the zero-shot ACCIDENT @ CVPR benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Decomposing zero-shot video understanding into temporal localization, semantic classification, and spatial grounding enables more reliable reasoning with vision-language models than direct prompting alone.

title: MFEN:Multi-Frequency Expert Network for Visible-Infrared Person Re-ID
summary: Visible-infrared person re-identification (VI-ReID) is challenging due to the large modality discrepancy between visible and infrared images. We contend that this discrepancy is largely related to differing lighting conditions, including differences in light wavelength and light source type. Recently, frequency-based VI-ReID approaches have achieved notable success because frequency information can better extract identity-relevant contours and details while excluding irrelevant lighting and color. However, existing methods either do not distinguish different frequency bands or focus on only one band, which is insufficient under diverse lighting conditions. To perform comprehensive frequency domain learning, we propose a Multi-Frequency Expert Network (MFEN) that enables multi-frequency modulation and adaptively combines different bands through a mixture-of-experts design. We further introduce Random Frequency Augmentation (RFA) and Frequency Auxiliary Optimization (FAO) to better train MFEN. The three modules are complementary and jointly capture critical frequency-domain details for robust representation learning. Extensive experiments on three VI-ReID datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach.

AI.tldr: MFEN uses multi-frequency experts with Random Frequency Augmentation and Frequency Auxiliary Optimization to tackle lighting-induced modality gap in VI-ReID, achieving strong results on three datasets.
AI.motivation: Visible-infrared person re-identification suffers from large modality discrepancy driven by lighting differences (wavelength and source). Frequency-domain information can emphasize identity-relevant contours/details while suppressing lighting/color. However prior work either uses a single frequency band or treats bands uniformly, limiting robustness under diverse lighting.
AI.method: Propose Multi-Frequency Expert Network (MFEN) with multiple frequency bands processed by separate experts and fused via a mixture-of-experts to adaptively combine bands. Introduce Random Frequency Augmentation (RFA) for diverse frequency perturbations and Frequency Auxiliary Optimization (FAO) for auxiliary supervision to guide frequency-domain learning. These components are jointly trained to capture comprehensive frequency-domain cues for robust representations.
AI.result: Three-module framework with MFEN,RFA,FAO yields improved and robust VI-ReID representations, with extensive experiments on three VI-ReID datasets demonstrating effectiveness over baselines and across varied lighting conditions.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging aligned multi-frequency representations with complementary training strategies provides a robust solution for VI-ReID under lighting variation, offering a general framework for frequency-domain learning in cross-modality re-identification.

title: Performance Analysis of YOLOv11 and YOLOv8 for Mixed Traffic Object Detection under Adverse Weather Conditions in Developing Countries
summary: In modern vehicular systems, robust performance under harsh conditions has become a critical problem of autonomous driving. Our study delivers a comprehensive evaluation of the newest iteration of the YOLO series, which is YOLOv11 Nano architecture benchmarked against the widely adopted YOLOv8 Nano as a baseline on a custom fused dataset that combines the Indian Driving Dataset (IDD) [1] and Berkeley Deep Drive Dataset (BDD100K) [2]. We have analyzed the trade-offs among detection accuracy, inference speed, and computational efficiency in high-entropy scenarios involving dense mixed traffic, rain, and low-light conditions. Specifically, YOLOv11n achieves a mean Average Precision (mAP@50) of 46.6%, with a notable 3.2% improvement in Precision over the baseline, effectively reducing false positives in cluttered scenes. Furthermore, the proposed model exhibits enhanced energy efficiency, requiring 22% fewer FLOPs (6.3G vs. 8.1G) while maintaining real-time inference speed of 70.9 FPS on a Tesla T4 GPU, offering an optimal trade-off for safety-critical edge deployment.

AI.tldr: YOLOv11 Nano on a fused IDD+BDD100K dataset outperforms YOLOv8 Nano in harsh driving conditions, delivering higher precision and lower compute with real-time inference.
AI.motivation: To evaluate the robustness and efficiency of the latest YOLO iteration (YOLOv11n) under challenging autonomous-driving scenarios and compare it with the established YOLOv8 Nano baseline on a representative, adversarial dataset.
AI.method: Benchmark YOLOv11 Nano vs. YOLOv8 Nano on a custom fused dataset combining IDD and BDD100K. Assess detection accuracy (mAP@50), precision, FLOPs, and FPS in high-entropy scenarios (dense traffic, rain, low light) on a Tesla T4.
AI.result: YOLOv11n achieves mAP@50 of 46.6%, with a 3.2 percentage-point precision improvement over the baseline. It uses 22% fewer FLOPs (6.3G vs. 8.1G) and maintains real-time performance at 70.9 FPS on a Tesla T4.
AI.conclusion: YOLOv11 Nano offers a favorable trade-off for safety-critical edge deployment: improved accuracy and precision with lower computational demand and real-time inference under adverse conditions.

title: Efficient Multinomial Logistic Bandit via Frequent Directions
summary: This paper studies efficient online algorithms for multinomial logistic bandits (MLogB), where the feedback distribution over $K+1$ outcomes follows a multinomial logistic model of $d$-dimensional action vectors. A representative UCB-type algorithm, OFUL-MLogB, achieves a regret bound of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(Kd\sqrt{T})$, but still requires $\mathcal{O}(K^3d^3)$ time and $\mathcal{O}(K^2d^2)$ space per round due to parameter estimation and optimistic reward construction, which is prohibitive in high-dimensional settings. To address this limitation, we propose EOFD-MLogB, which integrates frequent directions matrix sketching into OFUL-MLogB. By maintaining a low-rank SVD sketch of the accumulated Hessian, constrained online Newton updates in parameter estimation and $Kd \times K$ spectral-norm computations in the reward bonus are reduced to one-dimensional root-finding tasks and $K \times K$ eigenvalue computations, respectively. This yields dominant per-round time complexity $\mathcal{O}(Kd(m+K)^2)$ and space complexity $\mathcal{O}(Kd(m+K))$, where $m \ll d$ is the sketch size. We further prove a regret bound of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(Δ_T(Kd\lnΔ_T+m)\sqrt{T})$, where the sketching error factor $Δ_T$ is controlled by the $m$-truncated spectral tail of the Hessian. Thus, when the Hessian is approximately low-rank, the regret is close to that of OFUL-MLogB. Experiments validate the computational efficiency and competitive performance.

AI.tldr: EOFD-MLogB combines frequent-directions sketching with OFUL-MLogB to achieve scalable online multinomial logistic bandit learning: per-round complexity O(Kd(m+K)^2) and space O(Kd(m+K)) with a regret bound near OFUL-MLogB under low-rank Hessian.
AI.motivation: To address the cubic time and quadratic space of OFUL-MLogB in high dimensions, while preserving competitive regret.
AI.method: Introduce frequent directions-based low-rank sketch of the Hessian; constrained online Newton updates; reduced reward bonus computations to 1D root-finding and KxK eigenvalue steps; derive regret bound involving Δ_T controlled by Hessian's m-truncated tail.
AI.result: Dominant per-round time O(Kd(m+K)^2), space O(Kd(m+K)); regret bound ~ O~(Δ_T(Kd ln Δ_T + m) sqrt(T)); empirical validation shows efficiency and competitive performance.
AI.conclusion: EOFD-MLogB provides scalable, nearly optimal regret under low-rank Hessian; suitable for high-dimensional settings where OFUL-MLogB is computationally prohibitive.

title: PAWS: Preference Learning with Advantage-Weighted Segments
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICML 2026
summary: Preference-based reinforcement learning (PbRL) learns policies from human trajectory-level comparisons, avoiding explicit reward design and expert demonstrations. Existing methods typically train utility functions on trajectory or segment-level preferences while relying on per-step utility estimates during policy optimization. This training and inference mismatch induces a distribution shift that severely degrades temporal credit assignment and limits policy learning. We analyze this issue and propose PAWS, a segment-based preference learning method that performs policy updates directly using segment-level advantage functions. By aligning utility training with policy optimization, PAWS preserves trajectory-level preference information and avoids unreliable per-step learning signals. Experiments on simulated robotic manipulation and locomotion tasks demonstrate that PAWS consistently outperforms existing PbRL approaches, highlighting the importance of distribution-consistent preference learning.

AI.tldr: PAWS is a segment-based PbRL method that uses segment-level advantages to update policies, aligning utility training with policy optimization and outperforming prior PbRL methods on robotic tasks.
AI.motivation: A distribution shift arises because PbRL methods train utilities on whole trajectories but optimize policies using per-step signals, degrading temporal credit assignment and learning performance.
AI.method: PAWS introduces segment-based preference learning, updating the policy directly with segment-level advantage estimates, thereby matching training objectives with optimization signals.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on simulated robotic manipulation and locomotion shows PAWS consistently surpasses existing PbRL approaches, demonstrating the value of distribution-consistent preference learning.
AI.conclusion: Aligning preference training and policy optimization at the segment level improves accuracy of credit assignment and overall PbRL performance.

title: World Model Self-Distillation: Training World Models to Solve General Tasks
summary: Pretrained video generators are promising visual world models that exhibit emergent task-solving abilities; however, their reliance on detailed textual descriptions limits their direct use for planning and decision-making. Existing approaches either outsource this reasoning to language or vision-language models, or rely on supervised fine-tuning with paired task-execution videos, which are costly to collect and difficult to scale. We propose a scalable framework that elicits task-solving ability in such models by combining self-distillation with reinforcement learning. Given an unlabeled scene image, a vision-language model generates a candidate task and a detailed step-by-step solution. The solution conditions a pretrained video diffusion model, the Demonstrator; we distill its behavior into an Executor conditioned only on the image and a short task prompt. This transfers execution knowledge from caption-guided generation to instruction-conditioned task solving without curated task-video supervision.
